topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT721 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS & AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🤖 Module 1: Introduction to Robotics

## 1.1 Definition of a Robot

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (ISO 8373:2021):**
> A **robot** is an automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications.

The word **"Robot"** was coined by the Czech playwright **Karel Čapek** in his 1920 play *R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)*. It comes from the Slavic word *robota* meaning **"forced labor"** or **"drudgery."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A machine is *only* classified as a true robot if it satisfies ALL FOUR criteria:
> 1. **Programmable** — Can be re-tasked via software without physical rewiring.
> 2. **Multipurpose** — Can perform diverse tasks (welding, painting, picking, etc.).
> 3. **Automatic** — Operates with minimal or no human intervention.
> 4. **Three or more axes** — Possesses sufficient Degrees of Freedom (DOF) for spatial manipulation.

### 🌟 Intuitive Analogy — "The Robot as a Mechanical Human"

Imagine you hire a tireless, super-precise assistant in a factory. You give this assistant:
- **A brain** (Controller/Computer) → to think and decide
- **Eyes and ears** (Sensors) → to perceive the environment
- **Arms and joints** (Manipulator + Actuators) → to physically interact
- **Legs or wheels** (Mobile base, if applicable) → to move around

That assistant is essentially a **robot**. Unlike a human worker who may get tired, bored, or distracted, a robot performs repetitive or hazardous tasks with **constant precision, 24/7**. A car-welding robot on an assembly line, for instance, performs the *exact* same weld arc trajectory millions of times with sub-millimeter accuracy — something no human can sustain.

---

## 1.2 Historical Evolution of Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Favorite:** Timeline-based questions frequently appear in Part A (3-mark) sections.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Timeline of Robotics Milestones:} \\
1920 & : \text{Karel Čapek coins the word "Robot"} \\
1948 & : \text{Grey Walter builds "Elmer" and "Elsie" (turtle robots)} \\
1954 & : \text{George Devol patents the first programmable robot (Unimate)} \\
1961 & : \text{Unimate robot installed at GM — first industrial robot} \\
1968 & : \text{Stanford Arm (Victor Scheinman) — first electrically powered, articulated robot arm} \\
1973 & : \text{KUKA builds FAMULUS — first 6-axis electromechanical robot} \\
1978 & : \text{PUMA (Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly) developed by Unimation} \\
1999 & : \text{ASIMO by Honda — advanced humanoid robot} \\
2002 & : \text{Roomba by iRobot — first mass-market domestic robot} \\
2010s-2020s & : \text{Collaborative Robots (Cobots), AI-driven robotics, Soft robotics}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 1.3 Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> **Isaac Asimov (1942)** introduced these iconic laws in his science-fiction short story *"Runaround."* Although fictional, they form the philosophical foundation of **robot ethics** and are routinely asked in KTU exams.

$$\text{Law 1: A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.}$$

$$\text{Law 2: A robot must obey orders given by authorized humans, except where such orders conflict with Law 1.}$$

$$\text{Law 3: A robot must protect its own existence, as long as this does not conflict with Laws 1 or 2.}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **0th Law (Later Addition by Asimov):** *"A robot may not harm humanity as a whole, or by inaction allow humanity to come to harm."* Students often forget this — it supersedes the original three when applied at a global scale.

---

## 1.4 Difference Between Robotics and Automation

| Aspect | Automation | Robotics |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| **Scope** | Broader — encompasses any self-operating system | Subset focused on reprogrammable, physical machines |
| **Flexibility** | Often dedicated to one specific task | Easily reprogrammed for multiple tasks |
| **Intelligence Level** | May be purely mechanical (e.g., cams, timers) | Incorporates sensors, controllers, and decision logic |
| **Example** | A conveyor belt with a fixed stop | An articulated arm that welds, paints, and picks |
| **Core Element** | Process control | Physical manipulation |
| **Human Form** | None necessarily | Mimics human/movement tasks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** All robots *can* be part of an automated system, but **not all automation involves robots**. A thermostat is automation; a robotic arm is automation *plus* robotics.

---

## 1.5 Advantages and Disadvantages of Robots

### ✅ Advantages
- **Higher productivity** — operates continuously without fatigue
- **Superior precision** — repeatability down to **±0.02 mm** (microns)
- **Hazardous operation** — handles toxic, explosive, or extreme-temperature environments
- **Consistent quality** — eliminates human variability
- **Multitasking** — one robot can replace multiple machines

### ❌ Disadvantages
- **High initial capital investment**
- **Specialized maintenance** requires trained technicians
- **Limited adaptability** outside programmed parameters
- **Job displacement** — social and economic concern
- **Safety hazards** — requires rigorous guarding (cages, light curtains, interlocks)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Robot Anatomy & Classification

## 2.1 Basic Components of a Robot

A robot is fundamentally an integration of **six major subsystems**. Understanding each one is critical for KTU examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Mandate:** Module 1 expects students to *diagram and label* the basic components of a robot. Memorize the six subsystems below.

### 2.1.1 The Six Subsystems

1. **Mechanical Structure (Manipulator / Arm)**
   - The physical "body" — links, joints, and the end-effector
   - Made of steel, aluminum, or composite materials
   - Provides the skeletal framework

2. **Power Supply / Drive System**
   - Supplies energy to actuators
   - Common sources: **electricity (servo/stepper motors)**, **hydraulic fluid**, or **compressed air (pneumatics)**

3. **Actuators**
   - Convert energy into mechanical motion
   - Three primary types:
     - **Electric** (DC servo, stepper, brushless) — *most common in modern industry*
     - **Hydraulic** — high force, used in heavy-duty applications
     - **Pneumatic** — fast, clean, lower precision

4. **Controllers**
   - The "brain" of the robot — typically a **microcontroller, PLC, or industrial PC**
   - Executes programmed instructions and sends signals to actuators
   - Modern controllers use **ROS (Robot Operating System)** for advanced applications

5. **Sensors**
   - Provide feedback about internal state (encoders, potentiometers) and external environment (cameras, force/torque sensors, ultrasonic, LIDAR)

6. **End-Effector (End-of-Arm Tooling / EOAT)**
   - The tool that interacts with the workpiece
   - Examples: gripper, welding torch, paint sprayer, suction cup, drill

---

## 2.2 Robot Anatomy: Links, Joints, and Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

### 2.2.1 Links and Joints
- A **link** is a rigid body segment between two joints.
- A **joint** is the movable connection between two links.
- A joint provides either **linear (prismatic)** or **rotational (revolute)** motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Joint Types (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Revolute (R):** Rotational motion around an axis (like the human elbow)
> - **Prismatic (P):** Linear sliding motion (like an extending drawer)
> - **Cylindrical, Spherical, Screw, Planar** are combinations/derivatives of R and P.

### 2.2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

$$\boxed{\text{DOF} = \text{Number of independent movements required to specify the position and orientation of the end-effector in 3D space}}$$

For a robot to position and orient an object in **3-dimensional space**, it needs a minimum of **6 DOF**:
- **3 DOF for positioning** (x, y, z — location in Cartesian space)
- **3 DOF for orientation** (roll, pitch, yaw — rotation around x, y, z)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 6 DOF?** In 3D space, a rigid body has **6 independent parameters** — 3 translations + 3 rotations. Any robot with fewer than 6 DOF cannot reach *every* arbitrary pose.

### 2.2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------|---------------|
| Minimum DOF for spatial reach | $\text{DOF}_{\min} = 6$ | Dimensionless count |
| DOF of a planar robot | $\text{DOF} = 3 \times n_{links} - 2 \times n_{joints} - 3$ | Grübler's equation (planar) |
| DOF of a spatial robot | $\text{DOF} = 6 \times (n-1) - 5 \times j_1 - 4 \times j_2 - 3 \times j_3 - 2 \times j_4 - j_5$ | Spatial Kutzbach–Grübler |
| Workspace volume | $V = \iiint dV$ (integration over swept region) | m³ or mm³ |
| Repeatability | $R = \pm \sigma$ (standard deviation) | mm or μm |
| Payload capacity | $m_{\max}$ when reach $= L_{\max}$ | kg |
| Robot resolution | $\text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{Total travel}}{\text{Number of control increments}}$ | mm or deg |
| Speed | $v = \frac{d\theta}{dt}$ (rotational) or $v = \frac{ds}{dt}$ (linear) | rad/s or m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **accuracy** with **repeatability**.
> - **Accuracy:** How close the robot gets to a *commanded* position.
> - **Repeatability:** How consistently it returns to a *programmed* position over many cycles.
> Industrial robots typically have *high* repeatability but *moderate* accuracy.

---

## 2.3 Robot Coordinate Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic:** Every robot operates within a defined coordinate frame.

| Coordinate System | Symbol | Description | Common Application |
|-------------------|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| **World / Global** | $\{W\}$ | Fixed reference for the entire cell | Factory floor layout |
| **Base** | $\{B\}$ | Attached to the robot's base | Reference for all joint motions |
| **Tool / End-Effector** | $\{T\}$ | Origin at the tool tip | Pick-and-place, welding torch |
| **Object / Workpiece** | $\{O\}$ | Attached to the part being worked on | Vision-guided robotics |
| **Joint** | $\theta_i$ or $d_i$ | Per-joint angular or linear value | Internal servo control |

---

## 2.4 Classification of Robots

### 2.4.1 Classification by Mechanical Structure

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-asked classification** in KTU exams. Memorize all six types.

| Type | Description | DOF | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-----|-------------|
| **Cartesian (Gantry)** | Three prismatic joints along X, Y, Z | 3 | Pick-and-place, CNC, 3D printing |
| **Cylindrical** | One revolute + two prismatic joints | 3 | Assembly, machine loading |
| **Spherical (Polar)** | Two revolute + one prismatic joint | 3 | Welding, painting |
| **SCARA** | Two revolute (parallel) + one prismatic (vertical) | 3-4 | High-speed assembly, PCB |
| **Articulated (Revolute)** | All revolute joints (like human arm) | 5-6+ | Welding, painting, material handling |
| **Parallel (Delta)** | Multiple arms connecting to a single platform | 3-6 | High-speed pick-and-place (food, pharma) |

### 2.4.2 Classification by Application
- **Industrial Robots** — manufacturing, assembly, welding
- **Service Robots** — domestic, medical, hospitality
- **Mobile Robots** — AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), drones, AMRs
- **Humanoid Robots** — ASIMO, Atlas, Optimus
- **Medical Robots** — Da Vinci Surgical System
- **Exploration Robots** — Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance), underwater ROVs

### 2.4.3 Classification by Control Method
- **Servo-Controlled** — closed-loop, high accuracy
- **Non-Servo (Open-Loop)** — simpler, cheaper, lower accuracy
- **Point-to-Point (PTP)** — moves between discrete points without path control
- **Continuous Path (CP)** — follows a controlled trajectory (used in painting/welding)

---

## 2.5 Robot Work Envelope (Workspace)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **work envelope** is the total region in 3D space that the end-effector (or tool tip) can reach.

Two subtypes:
- **Dexterous workspace** — All *orientations* are achievable (typically smaller)
- **Reachable workspace** — All *positions* are reachable (typically larger)

For a **Cartesian robot** with arm lengths $L_x, L_y, L_z$:

$$\text{Workspace Volume} = L_x \times L_y \times L_z$$

For an **Articulated robot** with two equal links of length $L$ each:

$$V \approx \frac{4}{3}\pi L^3 \cdot (1 - \cos\theta_{\max})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Work envelope determines whether a robot can serve a particular machine in the factory layout. Under-specifying the envelope leads to unreachable positions and production downtime.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Degrees of Freedom Using Kutzbach–Grübler Criterion

The **Kutzbach–Grübler equation** is the gold-standard method to calculate the DOF of any mechanical linkage (including robots).

### 3.1.1 Planar Mechanism Equation

For a planar mechanism moving in 2D:

$$\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Where:
- $n$ = total number of links (including the ground link)
- $j_1$ = number of lower-pair joints (1 DOF each, e.g., pin, slider)
- $j_2$ = number of higher-pair joints (2 DOF each, e.g., cam, gear mesh)

### 3.1.2 Spatial Mechanism Equation (Used for Robots)

For a spatial (3D) mechanism:

$$\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

Where $j_1$ through $j_5$ are joints with $1, 2, 3, 4,$ and $5$ DOF respectively.

### 3.1.3 Worked Example — A 4-Bar Planar Linkage

**Problem:** A planar four-bar linkage has 4 links and 4 revolute joints (each 1 DOF).

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify parameters} \quad n &= 4, \quad j_1 = 4 \\
\text{Step 2: Apply the planar Kutzbach–Grübler equation} \quad \text{DOF} &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 \\
\text{Step 3: Substitute} \quad \text{DOF} &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) \\
\text{Step 4: Compute} \quad \text{DOF} &= 3 \times 3 - 2 \times 4 \\
\text{Step 5: Final result} \quad \text{DOF} &= 9 - 8 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A four-bar linkage has **1 DOF** — meaning that specifying the angle of *one* input link uniquely determines the position of every other link. This is the *Grashof condition* foundation.

### 3.1.4 Worked Example — SCARA Robot DOF

**Problem:** A SCARA robot has 4 revolute joints and 1 prismatic joint. Calculate its DOF using the spatial equation.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: List components} \quad n &= 5 \text{ links (including ground)} \\
j_1 &= 4 \text{ revolute joints (1 DOF each)} \\
j_2 &= 1 \text{ prismatic joint (1 DOF)} \\
\text{Step 2: Plug into spatial equation} \quad \text{DOF} &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5 \\
\text{Step 3: Substitute} \quad \text{DOF} &= 6(5-1) - 5(4) - 4(1) - 0 - 0 - 0 \\
\text{Step 4: Compute} \quad \text{DOF} &= 24 - 20 - 4 = 0 \\
\text{Step 5: Hmm — let us revise} \\
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Direct application of the spatial formula gives misleading results for *closed-loop* or *constrained* serial chains like SCARA. The **practical DOF** is obtained by counting movable joints: **3 in a basic SCARA**, or **4 if an additional rotation about the tool axis is present.**

$$\boxed{\text{For SCARA: DOF}_{\text{practical}} = n_{\text{revolute}} + n_{\text{prismatic}} = 3 \text{ (or 4 with Z-axis rotation)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Workspace of a 2-Link Planar Arm

Consider a 2-link planar manipulator (the simplest articulated arm) with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, both rotating freely.

### 3.2.1 Forward Kinematics

Let $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ be the joint angles. The end-effector position $(x, y)$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.2 Deriving Workspace Boundaries

**Minimum reach** (arm folded back on itself):

$$r_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$$

**Maximum reach** (arm fully extended):

$$r_{\max} = L_1 + L_2$$

Therefore, the workspace is an **annulus** (ring-shaped region):

$$\boxed{\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \le \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \le L_1 + L_2}$$

### 3.2.3 Worked Example

**Problem:** A 2-link planar robot has $L_1 = 5 \text{ cm}$ and $L_2 = 3 \text{ cm}$. Find the workspace area and whether the point $(6, 0)$ is reachable.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compute radii} \quad r_{\min} &= \vert 5 - 3 \vert = 2 \text{ cm} \\
r_{\max} &= 5 + 3 = 8 \text{ cm} \\
\text{Step 2: Compute workspace area (annulus)} \quad A &= \pi(r_{\max}^2 - r_{\min}^2) \\
\text{Step 3: Substitute} \quad A &= \pi(8^2 - 2^2) = \pi(64 - 4) = 60\pi \text{ cm}^2 \\
\text{Step 4: Compute numerically} \quad A &\approx 188.5 \text{ cm}^2 \\
\text{Step 5: Check reachability of point (6, 0)} \quad r &= \sqrt{6^2 + 0^2} = 6 \text{ cm} \\
\text{Step 6: Compare} \quad 2 \le 6 \le 8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Reachable} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Joint vs. Link Configuration for Major Robot Types

| Robot Type | Joint Sequence | DOF | Configuration Code (Joint Notation) | Cartesian Pose (x,y,z) Reach Equation |
|-----------|----------------|-----|--------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Cartesian | P-P-P | 3 | PPP | $x \in [0, L_x],\ y \in [0, L_y],\ z \in [0, L_z]$ |
| Cylindrical | R-P-P | 3 | RPP | $r = d_1,\ \theta = \theta_1,\ z = d_3$ |
| Spherical (Polar) | R-R-P | 3 | RRP | $r = d_1,\ \phi = \theta_2,\ \theta = \theta_1$ |
| SCARA | R-R-P | 3 | RRP (selectively compliant) | $(x,y)$ planar, $z = d_3$ |
| Articulated | R-R-R-R-R-R | 6 | RRRRRR | Nonlinear — uses forward kinematics |
| Delta (Parallel) | R-R-R + parallelogram links | 3 (translation only) | 3-DoF parallel | Cartesian translation within a dome |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Robot Resolution

**Definition:** The smallest increment of motion that the controller can command.

$$\boxed{\text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{Total range of motion}}{\text{Number of addressable increments}}}$$

### Worked Example

A joint has a range of $270°$ and the controller outputs 16-bit commands ($2^{16} = 65{,}536$ increments).

**Step-by-Step:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify parameters} \quad \text{Range} &= 270° \\
N &= 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \\
\text{Step 2: Apply the resolution formula} \quad R &= \frac{270°}{65{,}536} \\
\text{Step 3: Compute} \quad R &\approx 0.00412°/\text{increment} \\
\text{Step 4: Convert to arcseconds} \quad R &\approx 14.83 \text{ arcseconds}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher resolution = finer motion control.** A robot with 0.004° resolution is suitable for precision assembly, while a 1° resolution robot would suffice for heavy welding.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Major Subsystems of a Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[USER / PROGRAMMER] --> B[CONTROLLER]
    B --> C[POWER SUPPLY]
    C --> D[ACTUATORS]
    D --> E[MECHANICAL STRUCTURE]
    E --> F[END EFFECTOR]
    F --> G[WORKPIECE]
    H[SENSORS] --> B
    H --> E
    subgraph PERCEPTION
        H
    end
    subgraph DECISION
        B
    end
    subgraph ACTION
        C
        D
        E
        F
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> This **Sense → Plan → Act** loop is the cornerstone of robotic control. Note the **feedback arrow** from Sensors back to the Controller — this represents **closed-loop control**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Robot Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[ROBOTS] --> R1[BY MECHANICAL STRUCTURE]
    R0 --> R2[BY APPLICATION]
    R0 --> R3[BY CONTROL METHOD]
    R1 --> R1A[Cartesian PPP]
    R1 --> R1B[Cylindrical RPP]
    R1 --> R1C[Spherical RRP]
    R1 --> R1D[SCARA RRP]
    R1 --> R1E[Articulated RRRRRR]
    R1 --> R1F[Delta Parallel]
    R2 --> R2A[Industrial]
    R2 --> R2B[Service]
    R2 --> R2C[Mobile]
    R2 --> R2D[Humanoid]
    R2 --> R2E[Medical]
    R2 --> R2F[Exploration]
    R3 --> R3A[Servo Controlled]
    R3 --> R3B[Non Servo Open Loop]
    R3 --> R3C[Point to Point PTP]
    R3 --> R3D[Continuous Path CP]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Robot Arm Anatomy (6 DOF Articulated Robot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BASE LINK] -->|JOINT 1 Rotation around Z axis WAIST] B
    B -->|JOINT 2 Rotation around Y axis SHOULDER] C
    C -->|JOINT 3 Rotation around Y axis ELBOW] D
    D -->|JOINT 4 Rotation around X axis FOREARM ROLL] E
    E -->|JOINT 5 Rotation around Y axis WRIST PITCH] F
    F -->|JOINT 6 Rotation around X axis TOOL ROLL] G[END EFFECTOR]
    G --> H[TOOL FLANGE]
    H --> I[GRIPPER OR WELD TORCH]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Joint 1 (Waist):** Rotation about the vertical axis — sweeps the arm around.
> **Joint 2 (Shoulder):** Tilts the arm forward/backward.
> **Joint 3 (Elbow):** Extends or retracts the forearm.
> **Joints 4, 5, 6:** Combined motions of the wrist that orient the tool in roll, pitch, and yaw.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Sense → Plan → Act Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SENSE Sensors read environment] --> B[PLAN Controller computes trajectory]
    B --> C[ACT Actuators drive joints]
    C --> D[PERCEPTION RESULT Robot moves in workspace]
    D --> E[Feedback to sensors]
    E --> A
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style B fill:#B0E0E6
    style C fill:#98FB98
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Link:** This feedback loop embodies **closed-loop control**, distinguishing true robots from simple open-loop machines.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Robot Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[START What is your application] --> Q1{Heavy payload above 100 kg}
    Q1 -->|YES| HYD[Use HYDRAULIC Articulated Robot]
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2{Require high speed pick and place}
    Q2 -->|YES| DELTA[Use DELTA Parallel Robot]
    Q2 -->|NO| Q3{Need vertical insertion like PCB assembly}
    Q3 -->|YES| SCARA[Use SCARA Robot]
    Q3 -->|NO| Q4{Workspace is a simple rectangular box}
    Q4 -->|YES| CART[Use CARTESIAN Gantry Robot]
    Q4 -->|NO| Q5{Need complex 3D motion with orientation}
    Q5 -->|YES| ART[Use 6 DOF ARTICULATED Robot]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This decision tree mirrors actual robot procurement logic used by industrial automation engineers. Always match the *task geometry* to the *robot geometry*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a robot. State Asimov's laws of robotics. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A robot is an **automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator** programmable in three or more axes (ISO 8373). 

**Asimov's Three Laws:**
1. A robot may not injure a human being, or through inaction, allow harm.
2. A robot must obey human orders unless they conflict with Law 1.
3. A robot must protect its own existence unless it conflicts with Laws 1 or 2.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students forget to mention that the robot must be programmable in *three or more axes*. Without this phrase, the definition is incomplete and loses **1 mark**.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between robotics and automation. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Robotics | Automation |
|----------|-----------|
| Subset of automation | Broader concept |
| Involves reprogrammable physical machines | Can be purely mechanical (cams, timers) |
| Uses sensors and decision logic | May have no intelligence |
| Example: Robotic welding arm | Example: Conveyor with photo-eye stop |
| Focus on manipulation | Focus on process control |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You must answer **either Question A or Question B** in full.

---

### 🔹 Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the basic components of a robot with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The six major subsystems of a robot are:

1. **Mechanical Structure (Manipulator):** The physical body comprising links and joints; provides the skeletal framework. Made of steel/aluminum.

2. **Power Supply:** Provides electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy. Common: 3-phase AC mains, servo drives, hydraulic pumps, air compressors.

3. **Actuators:** Convert energy into motion:
   - **Electric (DC servo, stepper)** — most precise, used in modern industrial robots
   - **Hydraulic** — high force-to-weight ratio, for heavy loads
   - **Pneumatic** — fast, clean, lower precision

4. **Controller:** The "brain." Runs on a microcontroller/PLC/industrial PC. Executes the program and sends command signals to actuators. Uses feedback from encoders for closed-loop control.

5. **Sensors:** Provide perception:
   - **Internal:** Encoders, resolvers, potentiometers (joint position/speed)
   - **External:** Vision cameras, force/torque sensors, proximity, LIDAR

6. **End-Effector:** The tool mounted on the tool flange — gripper, welding torch, paint sprayer, vacuum cup, drill, etc.

A block diagram representation:

```
┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
│  Controller  │ →  │ Power Supply │ →  │  Actuators   │
└──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘
       ↑                                         ↓
       │                                   ┌──────────────┐
   ┌───────┐                               │  Mechanical  │
   │Sensors│  ← ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─  │  Structure   │
   └───────┘                               └──────────────┘
                                                  ↓
                                            ┌──────────────┐
                                            │ End-Effector │
                                            └──────────────┘
```

**(b)** Classify robots based on mechanical structure with diagrams and applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Joints | DOF | Geometry of Workspace | Typical Application |
|------|--------|-----|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Cartesian (Gantry)** | 3 Prismatic | 3 | Rectangular box | CNC, 3D printing, pick-and-place |
| **Cylindrical** | 1 Revolute + 2 Prismatic | 3 | Hollow cylinder | Machine loading, assembly |
| **Spherical (Polar)** | 2 Revolute + 1 Prismatic | 3 | Sphere section | Welding, painting |
| **SCARA** | 2 Revolute + 1 Prismatic | 3 | Flat disc + vertical | High-speed PCB assembly |
| **Articulated** | All Revolute | 5–6 | Sphere-like, irregular | Welding, painting, material handling |
| **Delta (Parallel)** | 3 Revolute + parallelogram struts | 3 | Dome above base | Ultra-fast pick-and-place (food, pharma) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often **confuse SCARA with Spherical robots.** Both use RRP, but SCARA has its two revolute joints on a *horizontal* plane and is *selectively compliant in the Z-axis* (hence the name). Spherical has vertical rotation.

---

### 🔹 Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the different types of joints and actuators used in robots. Compare electric, hydraulic, and pneumatic actuators. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Types of Joints:**

| Joint | Symbol | Motion | Example in Human Body |
|-------|--------|--------|----------------------|
| **Revolute (R)** | R | Rotation about an axis | Elbow, shoulder, wrist |
| **Prismatic (P)** | P | Linear sliding | Telescope extension |
| **Cylindrical** | C | Rotation + linear combined | None exactly |
| **Spherical (Ball-and-Socket)** | S | 3 rotational DOF | Hip joint |
| **Screw (Helical)** | H | Rotation + linear (coupled) | None exactly |
| **Planar** | E | 2 translations + 1 rotation | Wrist complex |

**Comparison of Actuators:**

| Parameter | Electric | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|-----------|----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Energy Source** | Electricity | Pressurized oil | Compressed air |
| **Force Output** | Medium–High | Very High | Low–Medium |
| **Speed** | High | Medium | Very High |
| **Precision** | Excellent (sub-mm) | Good | Moderate |
| **Cleanliness** | Clean | Risk of oil leaks | Very clean |
| **Maintenance** | Low | High (seals, filters) | Low |
| **Cost** | High initial | High initial + running | Lowest |
| **Typical Use** | Most industrial robots | Heavy payload, forging | Food, semiconductor |

**(b)** Define the following terms: (i) Degrees of Freedom, (ii) Work Envelope, (iii) Repeatability, (iv) Resolution, (v) Payload, (vi) Reach, (vii) Accuracy. **(7 Marks — 1 mark each)**

**Model Answers:**

1. **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent movements a robot can perform. Minimum 6 for full spatial manipulation.

2. **Work Envelope:** The 3D region in space that the robot's end-effector can reach.

3. **Repeatability:** The ability of a robot to return to a previously taught position repeatedly. Measured in mm (typically ±0.02 to ±0.1 mm).

4. **Resolution:** The smallest increment of motion the controller can command. Computed as Total Range ÷ Number of Increments.

5. **Payload:** The maximum weight a robot can carry while maintaining full performance, including the end-effector.

6. **Reach:** The maximum distance from the robot's base to the end-effector when fully extended.

7. **Accuracy:** How close the robot's actual position is to the *commanded* position. Distinct from repeatability.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently use **accuracy and repeatability interchangeably**. They are NOT the same. Repeatability is *consistency* (always returns to the same point). Accuracy is *correctness* (hits the commanded target).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Robot definition** — reprogrammable + multipurpose + automatic + ≥3 axes
- ✅ **Asimov's 4 Laws** — including the 0th Law (humanity)
- ✅ **Six components** — Manipulator, Power, Actuator, Controller, Sensor, End-effector
- ✅ **DOF minimum** — 6 for full spatial task (3 position + 3 orientation)
- ✅ **Six robot types** by structure — Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, SCARA, Articulated, Delta
- ✅ **Actuator types** — Electric (precision), Hydraulic (heavy duty), Pneumatic (fast & clean)
- ✅ **Joint types** — R, P, C, S, H, E
- ✅ **Repeatability ≠ Accuracy** — memorize the difference
- ✅ **Work envelope** — reachable vs. dexterous distinction
- ✅ **Kutzbach–Grübler formula** for DOF: $6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$
- ✅ **Resolution formula** = Total Range / Number of Increments
- ✅ **Automation ⊃ Robotics** — all robots are automation; not vice versa
- ✅ **SCARA joint notation** = RRP, NOT RRR (it is *selectively compliant*, not fully rigid)
- ✅ **First industrial robot** — Unimate, GM plant, 1961
- ✅ **PTP vs. CP control** — point-to-point vs. continuous path (painting, welding use CP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for 14-mark Questions:** Always include a **diagram** in answers worth more than 7 marks. KTU examiners award **1–2 free marks** for neat, labeled block diagrams even if your text is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT721 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS & AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Robot)**
> A **Robot** is a reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks. *(Source: Robot Institute of America — adopted as the KTU standard benchmark definition.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Robotics)**
> **Robotics** is the branch of mechanical engineering, electrical engineering, electronic engineering, and computer science that deals with the design, construction, operation, and application of **robots**, as well as computer systems for their control, sensory feedback, and information processing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Automation)**
> **Automation** is the technology by which a process or procedure is performed without human assistance. It is the use of control systems (such as computers, PLCs, CRTs, robots, etc.) to operate industrial machinery and processes, replacing human operators.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **chess-playing auto-player** vs. a **human chess grandmaster**:

- A **human grandmaster** (manual system) thinks, decides, and moves pieces with eyes and hands.
- A **chess computer engine** (basic automation) follows pre-coded rules and executes moves without fatigue.
- A **modern AI chess robot arm** (true robotics) **sees** the board (perception), **thinks** about the best move (intelligence), and **physically moves** the piece using a mechanical gripper (actuation).

A robot is essentially this trifecta — **Sensors (Eyes), Controller (Brain), and Actuators (Muscles)** — wrapped into one reprogrammable machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module-1 "Introduction" expects students to master the **definitions, Asimov's Three Laws, generations, classifications, and the fundamental differences between Robotization and Automation**. These are recurrent 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

### Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics (Mandatory KTU Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Three Laws of Robotics, introduced by **Isaac Asimov** in his 1942 short story *"Runaround"*, are mandatory for the KTU ESE. They form the ethical backbone of modern industrial and humanoid robotics.

| # | Law | Plain Meaning |
|---|-----|---------------|
| **Law 0** *(added later)* | A robot may not harm humanity, or, by inaction, allow humanity to come to harm. | Protect the species. |
| **Law 1** | A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm. | A human's safety is supreme. |
| **Law 2** | A robot must obey the orders given by human beings except where such orders conflict with the First Law. | Obedience to humans, unless harmful. |
| **Law 3** | A robot must protect its own existence, as long as that does not conflict with the First or Second Law. | Self-preservation is the lowest priority. |

### Key Performance Metrics You Must Memorize

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The **number of independent movements** a robot can perform. Standard industrial manipulator = **6 DOF** (3 for position + 3 for orientation).
- **Payload:** Maximum weight a robot can carry while maintaining rated performance, expressed in **kg** or **N**.
- **Reach (Work Envelope):** The 3-D spatial region the robot's end-effector can reach, measured in **mm or m**.
- **Repeatability:** The robot's ability to return to a taught position; precision metric, **±0.02 mm** for high-precision robots.
- **Accuracy:** The deviation between the commanded position and the actual attained position.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Robot — The Core Subsystems

Every industrial robot, regardless of brand (FANUC, KUKA, ABB, Yaskawa), is composed of **four fundamental subsystems**:

1. **Mechanical Structure (Manipulator / Arm)**
   - Comprises links, joints, and the base.
   - **Joints** are either **Revolute (R)** — rotational, like a hinge — or **Prismatic (P)** — linear sliding.
   - Standard KUKA/ABB arm: **RRR** configuration (all-rotational joints).

2. **Drive / Actuation System**
   - Provides the mechanical power to the joints.
   - Common types: **Electric (servo/stepper motors)**, **Hydraulic (high payload > 500 kg)**, **Pneumatic (low-cost, simple pick-and-place)**.

3. **Controller (The "Brain")**
   - Microprocessor/PLC-based unit that interprets the program and commands the drives.
   - Modern controllers integrate **PLC, motion controller, and HMI** in a single cabinet.

4. **Sensory / Feedback System**
   - **Internal sensors:** Encoders, resolvers, tachometers (joint position/velocity).
   - **External sensors:** Vision cameras, force/torque sensors, proximity sensors, ultrasonic, LIDAR.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the standard KTU board-validated formulas and symbolic metrics. Use this as your **last-night cheat sheet**.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Degrees of Freedom (Planar) | $n = 3(l - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $l$ = links, $j_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $j_2$ = 2-DOF joints |
| 2 | Degrees of Freedom (Spatial) | $M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | **Grübler-Kutzbach criterion** |
| 3 | Work Envelope (cylindrical) | $V = \pi r^2 h$ | $r$ = reach, $h$ = vertical travel |
| 4 | Work Envelope (spherical) | $V = \tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3$ | spherical manipulator |
| 5 | Resolution | $\text{Res} = \dfrac{\text{Total Travel}}{\text{Number of Pulses}}$ | mm per pulse |
| 6 | Repeatability | $R = \pm \dfrac{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}{2}$ | **mm**, at the tool tip |
| 7 | Stiffness | $k = \dfrac{F}{\delta}$ | **N/m**, structural rigidity |
| 8 | Cycle Time | $t_c = t_{\text{move}} + t_{\text{process}} + t_{\text{return}}$ | **seconds per cycle** |
| 9 | Payload Acceleration Limit | $F = m \cdot a$ | **Newton**, payload mass $\times$ acceleration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Grübler–Kutzbach Criterion (Spatial DOF)** is the most tested formula. For a typical **6-DOF industrial articulated robot**, $M = 6$ confirms the design.

### 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering

- **DOF equations** decide the **kinematic configuration** before fabrication.
- **Work envelope** determines **cell layout** in a factory — if the robot is undersized, it cannot service the workstation.
- **Repeatability** drives **tool selection** — for PCB assembly, $\pm 0.02$ mm is mandatory; for palletizing, $\pm 5$ mm is acceptable.
- **Cycle Time** dictates **ROI calculations** for management approvals.

### 2.4 Brief Generational Evolution of Robots

| Generation | Era | Distinguishing Feature | Example |
|------------|-----|------------------------|---------|
| **1st** | 1950s–60s | **Pick-and-place** (limited sensory feedback) | Unimate (1961, GM) |
| **2nd** | 1970s–80s | **Sensory feedback** (vision, force) | Cincinnati Milacron T3 |
| **3rd** | 1990s–2000s | **Intelligent / vision-guided** with off-line programming | ABB IRB series |
| **4th** | 2010s–20s | **Cobots + AI/ML integration**, mobile manipulation | Universal Robots UR10e, Boston Dynamics Spot |
| **5th** | 2025+ | **Cognitive / autonomous reasoning** (LLM-driven, self-learning) | Tesla Optimus, Figure 02 |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Grübler–Kutzbach Criterion for Spatial DOF

This is the single most important derivation in Module 1, regularly appearing for **7–14 marks** in KTU ESE.

**Step 1 — Statement of the General Formula**

For a planar mechanism, every link moving in a plane has **3 degrees of freedom** (2 translations + 1 rotation). The general Kutzbach mobility formula is:

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

**Step 2 — Extending to 3-D Space**

For a spatial mechanism, each unconstrained rigid body in 3-D space has **6 degrees of freedom** (3 translations + 3 rotations). Replacing the coefficient `3` with `6` and expanding the joint constraints, the spatial form becomes:

$$M = 6(n - 1) - 5 j_1 - 4 j_2 - 3 j_3 - 2 j_4 - j_5$$

where each $j_k$ denotes the number of joints with $k$ degrees of freedom.

**Step 3 — Meaning of Each Coefficient**

- $6(n-1)$: Total DOF of all links if they were free-floating in 3-D space.
- $5j_1$: Each 1-DOF joint (revolute or prismatic) removes **5 DOF**.
- $4j_2$: Each 2-DOF joint (e.g., universal) removes **4 DOF**.
- $3j_3$: Each 3-DOF joint (e.g., spherical) removes **3 DOF**, and so on.

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example**

For a **standard 6-DOF articulated robot** with 6 revolute joints and 6 links:

$$M = 6(6-1) - 5(6) - 4(0) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 0$$

$$M = 6 \times 5 - 30$$

$$M = 30 - 30 = 0$$

Wait — for an **open kinematic chain**, the Kutzbach formula above applies to **closed chains**. For an **open chain**, the correct form is:

$$M = 6(n - 1) - 5 j_1$$

$$M = 6(6-1) - 5(6) = 30 - 30 = 0$$

This is because an open chain with $n$ links and $n-1$ joints trivially has $M = 0$ degrees of freedom as a mechanism but $n-1$ **input DOF** for the robot. Hence the **true robot DOF = number of actuated joints = 6**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "calculate DOF", state the chain type (open/closed), the formula, substitute, and conclude with the physical meaning. Marks are awarded for **justification**, not just the number.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Robot vs. Automation vs. CNC

This comparison matrix is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question**. Memorize the differences.

| Parameter | **Robot** | **CNC Machine** | **Automation (Hard)** |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|------------------------|
| **Mobility** | Multi-axis, can move freely in 3-D | Restricted to linear/rotary toolpaths | Fixed sequence, no mobility |
| **Programming** | Off-line / lead-through / teach pendant | G-code / M-code (part-program) | Hard-wired relays / ladder logic |
| **Flexibility** | High — one robot, many tasks | Medium — part-family oriented | Very low — single dedicated task |
| **Sensors** | Internal + external feedback | Mostly limited (tool, workpiece) | Minimal (limit switches, sensors) |
| **Payload** | 0.5 kg – 1500 kg (varies) | Workpiece + fixture mass | Depends on conveyor / actuator |
| **Cost** | High (₹ 5 L – ₹ 1 Cr) | Medium-High | Low-Medium |
| **DOF** | Typically 4–7 | 3–5 axis | 0–2 |
| **Human Intervention** | Low (cobot = very low) | Low (operator loading) | Very low (fully automatic) |
| **Best Use** | Welding, painting, assembly | Milling, turning, grinding | Bottling, packaging, sorting |

### 3.3 Numerical Practice: Work Envelope Calculation

A **SCARA robot** has a horizontal reach of **600 mm** and a vertical stroke of **200 mm**.

The cylindrical work envelope is:

$$V = \pi r^2 h = \pi (0.6)^2 (0.2) = \pi \times 0.36 \times 0.2 = 0.0720 \pi \; \text{m}^3$$

$$V \approx 0.2262 \; \text{m}^3$$

Therefore, the SCARA can service any point within a cylinder of **radius 0.6 m** and **height 0.2 m**, which is suitable for **PCB assembly** and **pick-and-place** tasks in electronics manufacturing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Robot Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    U[User / Operator] -->|Teach Pendant| CTRL
    PROG[Off-line Programming PC] -->|Ethernet - TCP IP| CTRL
    CTRL[Robot Controller - The Brain] -->|Servo Pulses| DRV
    DRV[Drive System - Servo / Stepper Motors] -->|Mechanical Power| MECH
    MECH[Mechanical Manipulator - Links + Joints] -->|Motion| EE
    MECH -->|Joint Motion| SENS
    SENS[Internal Sensors - Encoders / Resolvers] -->|Feedback| CTRL
    EE[End Effector - Gripper / Welder / Spray Gun] -->|Task Execution| W
    W[Workpiece / Environment] -->|Interaction| ESENS
    ESENS[External Sensors - Vision / Force / Proximity] -->|Perception| CTRL
    CTRL -->|Status Display| HMI[HMI - Touchscreen Panel]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Data flows **bidirectionally** between the Controller and the manipulator. The Controller acts as the central node connecting programming inputs, drives, internal sensors, and external perception. **The Controller is the only element that receives feedback from both sensor types**, which is why it is called the "brain".

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — KTU Automation / Robotics Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L5[L5 - Enterprise Level - ERP - MES] --> L4
    L4[L4 - Plant Level - SCADA - Production Scheduling] --> L3
    L3[L3 - Cell Level - Cell Controller - PLC] --> L2
    L2[L2 - Workstation Level - Robot Controller - CNC - HMI] --> L1
    L1[L1 - Field Level - Sensors - Actuators - Motors - Drives]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model** — This 5-layer hierarchy is mandatory reading. **Robots occupy Layer 2 (Workstation)**, while **PLCs occupy Layer 3 (Cell)**. A typical KTU question maps a real industrial component (e.g., FANUC robot, Siemens PLC) onto its correct layer.

### 4.3 Mermaid — Asimov Laws Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Receives Command or Action Trigger] --> B{Does It Harm a Human?}
    B -->|Yes| X1[STOP - Refuse Action - Law 1 Overrides All]
    B -->|No| C{Does It Conflict With Human Order?}
    C -->|Yes| X2[Refuse - Law 2 Violated - Maintain Higher Priority]
    C -->|No| D{Does It Endanger the Robot Itself?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Weigh Against Law 1 and Law 2 - If No Conflict - Law 3 Applies]
    D -->|No| F[Execute Command - All Laws Satisfied]
    E --> G{Does Self-Preservation Conflict With Law 1 or 2?}
    G -->|Yes| X3[Sacrifice Itself - Higher Law Wins]
    G -->|No| F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Priority order:** Law 0 (humanity) > Law 1 (human) > Law 2 (obedience) > Law 3 (self). This priority cascade is the exact structure KTU board expects in a 7-mark explanation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Module 1 Introduction**
> - Students **lose 2–3 marks** by writing "robot is a machine" without the exact **Robot Institute of America (RIA) definition**.
> - Many confuse **Robot vs. CNC** — they are **not synonyms**; refer to the analytical matrix in Section 3.2.
> - In Asimov's Laws questions, students forget the **priority order**. Always state: *Law 1 > Law 2 > Law 3*, and mention **Law 0** if introduced.
> - DOF numericals: forgetting to declare the **chain type (open vs closed)** results in **-2 marks** in the KTU 2024 scheme.
> - In the work-envelope question, units must be **SI-consistent** (m, not mm) inside the formula. Convert before substituting.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a robot and list the four main subsystems of any industrial robot.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A robot is a **reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator** designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions to perform a variety of tasks *(Robot Institute of America definition — 2 marks)*.
The four main subsystems are:
1. **Mechanical Manipulator** (links + joints),
2. **Drive / Actuation System** (motors),
3. **Controller** (CPU + memory + power electronics),
4. **Sensory System** (internal encoders + external vision/force sensors) *(1 mark for the list)*.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics. Which law has the highest priority?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Law 1:** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm *(1 mark)*.
2. **Law 2:** A robot must obey human orders except where they conflict with Law 1 *(1 mark)*.
3. **Law 3:** A robot must protect its own existence, except where it conflicts with Laws 1 or 2 *(1 mark — also write priority: Law 1 > Law 2 > Law 3)*.

> **Highest Priority:** Law 1 (followed by Law 0 — the human species — if introduced).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QN. 1(a)/(b) Set]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of robots by generation**, highlighting the major technological leap in each generation. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **major components of an industrial robot system** and explain the role of the **controller**. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) Classification by Generation (7 Marks):**

| Generation | Decade | Technology Leap | Limitation |
|------------|--------|------------------|-------------|
| **1st** | 1950s–60s | Hydraulic, simple pick-and-place, no sensors | Blind, single-task |
| **2nd** | 1970s–80s | Microprocessor-based controllers, sensory feedback | Limited AI |
| **3rd** | 1990s | Vision, off-line programming, networked | Expensive |
| **4th** | 2010s | Cobots, AI/ML, mobile manipulators, ROS | Battery / safety |
| **5th** | 2025+ | Cognitive AI, LLM-driven, self-learning autonomy | Ethical regulation |

**Conclusion (½ mark):** Modern manufacturing transitions from 1st–2nd to 4th-gen cobots for human–robot collaboration.

---

**(b) Industrial Robot Block Diagram & Controller Role (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (already provided in Section 4.1 above — 3 marks for correct diagram with labels).**

**Controller Role (4 marks):**
1. **Path Planning:** Computes the trajectory for the end-effector from start to goal pose.
2. **Inverse Kinematics:** Solves joint angles $\theta_i$ from the desired Cartesian pose $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$.
3. **Servo Loop Closure:** Reads encoder feedback at **1–8 kHz** and adjusts PWM signals to the drives.
4. **Safety Interlocks:** Implements E-Stop, zone monitoring, and ISO 10218 / ISO/TS 15066 collaborative-mode limits.
5. **I/O Management:** Communicates with PLCs, conveyors, vision systems, and HMI via **EtherCAT, PROFINET, or DeviceNet**.

> **Conclusion (½ mark):** The controller is the single point of intelligence — without it, the manipulator is an inert mechanism.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QN. 2(a)/(b) Set]*

**(a)** Compare **robotics with automation**, listing at least **six technical differences**. Explain why a modern automotive assembly line uses a **combination of both**. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A 6-DOF articulated robot is to be installed in a **welding cell**. The work envelope is **spherical** with a reach of **1.5 m**. Calculate the **work envelope volume** and explain its **practical significance** in cell layout planning. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) Robotics vs Automation — 6 Technical Differences (7 Marks):**

| # | Robotics | Automation |
|---|----------|------------|
| 1 | Reprogrammable for multiple tasks | Fixed hard-wired sequence |
| 2 | Multi-DOF (typically 4–7) | 0–2 DOF, single-purpose |
| 3 | Sensory-rich (vision, force) | Limited sensors |
| 4 | High initial cost, high ROI on volume | Lower cost, simpler integration |
| 5 | Handles geometric variability | Handles identical parts only |
| 6 | Suitable for unstructured environments | Best in structured, repetitive lines |

**Why combine them? (2 marks):** In an automotive line, **hard automation** (e.g., conveyor, transfer line) ensures **high-volume, repeatable body-panel transfer**, while **robots** perform **welding, painting, and assembly** on variable models. This **hybrid system** offers the **throughput of automation** with the **flexibility of robotics** — a textbook example of the **Toyota Production System**.

---

**(b) Work Envelope Calculation (7 Marks):**

Given: $r = 1.5 \; \text{m}$, spherical envelope.

$$V = \tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3$$

$$V = \tfrac{4}{3} \pi (1.5)^3 = \tfrac{4}{3} \pi \times 3.375 = 4.5 \pi \; \text{m}^3$$

$$V \approx 14.137 \; \text{m}^3$$

**Practical Significance (3 marks):**
- The **welding torch** can reach any point inside this sphere.
- The **cell layout** must ensure that the **car body** is positioned within the sphere, otherwise the robot cannot service every weld joint.
- For a **typical car body of $4.8 \; \text{m} \times 1.8 \; \text{m} \times 1.5 \; \text{m}$**, a single robot is insufficient — a **dual-robot cell with overlapping envelopes** is deployed.
- Floor space occupied by the cell = footprint of the envelope + **safety exclusion zone (0.5 m buffer)** = $\pi (2.0)^2 \approx 12.57 \; \text{m}^2$.

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** The spherical envelope determines **cell footprint, robot count, and floor-space ROI**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Night KTU Module-1 Revision Checklist**

- **RIA Definition of Robot** — exact wording earns full marks.
- **Asimov's Laws** — write in **priority order**; mention Law 0 for humanity if the question allows.
- **Four Subsystems of a Robot** — Mechanical, Drive, Controller, Sensor.
- **DOF Formula (Spatial):** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ (Grübler–Kutzbach).
- **Work Envelope** — **Spherical**: $V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$, **Cylindrical**: $V = \pi r^2 h$.
- **Repeatability vs Accuracy** — Repeatability is the **ability to return**; Accuracy is the **ability to reach the commanded position**.
- **Generations of Robots** — 1st (blind), 2nd (sensory), 3rd (vision), 4th (cobot + AI), 5th (cognitive).
- **Robot vs CNC vs Automation** — Use the 9-row analytical matrix in Section 3.2 verbatim.
- **ISA-95 Levels** — Robots = L2, PLC = L3, SCADA = L4, ERP = L5.
- **Typical 6-DOF industrial robot** — All **revolute joints (RRR)**, payload 0.5–1500 kg, repeatability $\pm 0.02$ mm.
- **Mnemonic for Joints:** **R**evolute = **R**otation, **P**rismatic = **P**ush/pull.
- **Always state units** — KTU valuation deducts marks for missing SI units.
- **Diagram Drawings** — Always draw **labeled blocks with arrows**; KTU gives 2–3 marks separately for neat diagrams.
- **Hybrid cells** — Modern factories use **Robotics + Hard Automation** together — quote **Toyota / Tesla** as a real-world example.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Structure, Classification of Robot and Robotic System, Law of Robotics, Robot Motion, Wrist Configuration, Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT721 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS & AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Structure, Classification of Robot and Robotic System, Law of Robotics, Robot Motion, Wrist Configuration, Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Basic Structure of a Robot

### Formal Definition (KTU/ISO/RIA Standard)

A **robot** is a reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **ISO (International Organization for Standardization) Definition:** An industrial robot is an "automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Qualifiers (Board Exam Highlight):**
> 1. **Reprogrammable** — behavior can be changed without physical alteration.
> 2. **Multifunctional** — capable of performing multiple tasks.
> 3. **Manipulator** — physically interacts with the environment.
> 4. **Three or more axes** — distinguishes a robot from a simple mechanism.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Robot as a Mechanical Human

Imagine a robot as a **mechanical human working at a factory bench**:

| Human Body Part | Robot Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Brain | **Controller** | Decision making, sequencing |
| Eyes / Ears | **Sensors** | Perceive environment |
| Skeleton | **Mechanical Structure (Links)** | Provide support |
| Shoulder / Elbow | **Joints** | Allow rotation |
| Arm | **Arm / Manipulator** | Reach workspace |
| Hand | **End-Effector (Gripper)** | Hold/operate objects |
| Nerves | **Cables & Wiring** | Signal/power transmission |
| Muscles | **Actuators (Motors/Pneumatics)** | Produce motion |

This analogy makes the robot's anatomy instantly intuitive — every component has a **sensory role, a decision role, or an actuation role**.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Robots

### A. Classification by **Generation / Technology**

| Generation | Description | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **1st Gen — Playback Robot** | Records and replays taught motions | No external sensing |
| **2nd Gen — Sensory Robot** | Uses feedback from sensors | Adapts to environment |
| **3rd Gen — Intelligent Robot** | Uses AI, vision, decision-making | Cognitive ability |
| **4th Gen — Collaborative (Cobot)** | Works safely alongside humans | Human-robot interaction |

### B. Classification by **Coordinate System / Kinematic Structure**

This is the **highest-weightage classification in KTU exams** (covered later in Section 2).

1. **Cartesian (3P)** — PPP joints
2. **Cylindrical (R2P)** — RPP joints
3. **Spherical / Polar (2RP)** — RRP joints
4. **Articulated / Revolute (3R)** — RRR joints
5. **SCARA** — Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm
6. **Parallel (Delta)** — multiple arms meeting at one end-effector

### C. Classification by **Application**

- **Industrial Robots** — welding, painting, assembly, palletizing.
- **Service Robots** — domestic, medical, agricultural.
- **Mobile Robots** — AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), drones.
- **Humanoid Robots** — bipedal anthropomorphic design.

### D. Classification by **Drive / Actuation Technology**

- **Hydraulic Robots** — high payload (e.g., >500 kg), used in heavy industry.
- **Pneumatic Robots** — simple pick-and-place, low payload.
- **Electric Robots** — precision work, **most common in modern industry**.

---

## 1.3 Robotic System — Block-Level Architecture

A complete robotic system comprises **four functional blocks**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4 Pillars of a Robotic System (frequently asked 3-mark question):**
>
> 1. **Manipulator / Mechanical Unit** — links, joints, end-effector.
> 2. **Controller** — CPU, memory, servo amplifiers.
> 3. **Power Supply / Drive System** — hydraulic, pneumatic, or electric actuators.
> 4. **Sensors & Feedback** — position, velocity, vision, force/torque sensors.

---

## 1.4 Law of Robotics — Asimov's Three Laws

**Issac Asimov** formulated the **Three Laws of Robotics** in his 1942 short story *"Runaround"*. These are **fictional ethical laws** that govern robot behavior:

> [!NOTE]
> **Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics (Ranked by Priority):**
>
> **Law 1:** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
>
> **Law 2:** A robot must obey the orders given by human beings, except where such orders conflict with the First Law.
>
> **Law 3:** A robot must protect its own existence, as long as this does not conflict with the First or Second Law.

### Later Added (Zeroth Law)
> A robot may not harm humanity, or, through inaction, allow humanity to come to harm. *(Added in 1985; supersedes Laws 1-3)*

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of Asimov's laws as a **priority queue in a software controller**: Law 1 is the highest interrupt, Law 2 is medium-priority, Law 3 is low-priority. The robot's controller constantly checks these priorities before executing any action.

---

## 1.5 Robot Motion — Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

### Formal Definition
The **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a robot is the **number of independent movements** the manipulator can perform in 3D space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total DOF of a typical industrial robot = 6**
> - **3 DOF for positioning** (X, Y, Z translation)
> - **3 DOF for orientation** (roll, pitch, yaw)

### Conceptual Analogy
A human arm has **7 DOF**: shoulder (3) + elbow (1) + wrist (3). This redundancy allows us to reach a cup while keeping our eyes level. A robot with **6 DOF** is non-redundant but adequate for most industrial tasks.

---

## 1.6 Wrist Configuration

### Formal Definition
The **wrist** is the **end segment of the robot manipulator** that holds the end-effector. It provides **orientation** to the end-effector (the **last 3 DOF** of a typical 6-DOF robot).

### Standard Convention
Wrist motion is described by three **rotational joint axes** intersecting at a **common point** called the **wrist center**.

| Wrist Joint | Motion | KTU Nomenclature |
|---|---|---|
| Joint 4 | Bend (Pitch) | **B** |
| Joint 5 | Twist (Yaw) | **T** |
| Joint 6 | Swivel (Roll) | **S** |

A typical notation is **B-T-S**, **B-V-S**, **B-S-L**, etc., depending on joint axis arrangement.

### Conceptual Analogy
The wrist is like the **knuckle joint of your hand** — once your arm reaches a cup, your wrist tilts and rotates the cup to the exact orientation needed to drink. The wrist **does not change position**, only **orientation**.

---

## 1.7 Motion Control Types

| Motion Type | Full Form | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **PTP** | Point-to-Point | Moves between taught points; path between is uncontrolled |
| **CP** | Continuous Path | Both endpoints and path are controlled (e.g., welding, painting) |
| **Controlled Path** | Similar to CP | Path is explicitly specified by many intermediate points |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Tip:** PTP is used for **pick-and-place** operations. CP is used for **welding, painting, and gluing** where the path shape matters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Robot — Detailed Breakdown

### Manipulator Subsystem
The manipulator consists of a series of **rigid bodies (links)** connected by **joints**.

**Link:** A rigid structural member between two joints.
**Joint:** A connection that provides motion between two links.

### Six Types of Robotic Joints

| Joint Type | Symbol | Motion | Degrees of Freedom |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Revolute (R)** | R | Rotation about an axis | 1 |
| **Prismatic (P)** | P | Linear sliding along an axis | 1 |
| **Helical / Screw (H)** | H | Rotation + translation combined | 1 |
| **Cylindrical (C)** | C | Rotation + translation (coupled) | 2 |
| **Spherical (S)** | S | Rotation about 3 axes at one point | 3 |
| **Planar (E)** | E | Translation in 2 axes + rotation perpendicular | 3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exams:** Industrial robots mostly use **Revolute (R)** and **Prismatic (P)** joints only. A 6-DOF robot almost always has a combination of R and P joints.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Classification — Coordinate System Architectures

### 1. Cartesian (Gantry) Robot — **PPP**

- 3 prismatic joints aligned with **X, Y, Z** axes.
- **Work Envelope:** Rectangular cuboid.
- **Advantages:** High accuracy, simple kinematics, large workspace.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires large floor space.
- **Applications:** Pick-and-place, CNC loading, assembly.

### 2. Cylindrical Robot — **RPP**

- 1 revolute joint (base rotation) + 2 prismatic (vertical and radial).
- **Work Envelope:** Cylindrical volume.
- **Applications:** Machine tool tending, spot welding.

### 3. Spherical (Polar) Robot — **RRP**

- 2 revolute joints (base rotation, shoulder pivot) + 1 prismatic (radial extension).
- **Work Envelope:** Spherical segment.
- **Applications:** Heavy-duty material handling, forging.

### 4. Articulated (Revolute / Anthropomorphic) Robot — **RRR**

- 3 revolute joints — base, shoulder, elbow.
- **Work Envelope:** Roughly spherical, with irregular inner cavity.
- **Most common industrial robot** (e.g., ABB IRB, KUKA KR series).

### 5. SCARA Robot — **RRP** (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm)

- 2 revolute + 1 prismatic (vertical).
- **Work Envelope:** Cylindrical but with selective compliance (rigid in Z, compliant in XY).
- **Applications:** PCB assembly, high-speed pick-and-place.

### 6. Parallel (Delta) Robot

- Multiple arms connect the base to a single end-effector platform.
- **Work Envelope:** Small dome-shaped region.
- **Applications:** Ultra-fast packaging, food sorting.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **board-exam critical**. Master each one.

### Formula 1: Degrees of Freedom of a Robot

$$DOF = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i$$

where $f_i$ is the number of DOF contributed by joint $i$, and $n$ is the total number of joints.

### Formula 2: Grübler's Equation (for planar / spatial linkages)

For a **planar mechanism**:

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$$

For a **spatial mechanism**:

$$M = 6(n-1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5$$

where:
- $M$ = mobility (DOF) of the mechanism
- $n$ = number of links (including ground)
- $J_1$ = number of lower-pair joints (1 DOF each)
- $J_2$ = number of higher-pair joints (2 DOF each)

### Formula 3: Work Envelope Volumes

| Robot Type | Envelope Shape | Volume Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Cartesian | Rectangular cuboid | $V = 2X \cdot 2Y \cdot 2Z$ |
| Cylindrical | Cylinder + annular region | $V = \pi \left(R_{\max}^2 - R_{\min}^2\right) \cdot H$ |
| Spherical | Spherical segment | $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi R^3 (1 - \cos\theta)$ |
| Articulated | Irregular sphere | Approximated only |

### Formula 4: Resolution

$$Resolution = \frac{Range\ of\ Motion}{2^n}$$

where $n$ is the **number of bits** used in the encoder/control system.

### Formula 5: Repeatability vs Accuracy

- **Accuracy:** How close the robot reaches a *commanded* position.
- **Repeatability:** How close the robot returns to a *previously taught* position.

> **Industrial Reality:** Robots are usually **more repeatable than accurate**.

$$Accuracy = Resolution + Repeatability$$

### Formula 6: Payload Capacity

$$P_{max} = \frac{\tau_{motor} - \tau_{friction}}{\mu \cdot g \cdot L}$$

where $\tau$ is torque, $\mu$ is mass coefficient, $L$ is link length.

---

## 2.4 Wrist Configuration — Detailed Kinematics

### The Wrist Center Concept

The wrist center is the **common intersection point** of the last three joint axes. For a proper spherical wrist, all three axes must meet at this point.

### Standard Wrist Configurations (Common KTU Question)

| Configuration | Notation | Joint Axes | Special Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bend-Twist-Swivel** | B-T-S | All orthogonal | Most general purpose |
| **Bend-Swivel-Twist** | B-S-T | First two parallel | Improved singularity avoidance |
| **Bend-Volute-Swivel** | B-V-S | Middle joint has roll | Used in precision assembly |
| **Universal-Universal** | U-U | Two U-joints in series | Compact, low stiffness |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** Be prepared to draw or describe a wrist configuration from a written description (e.g., "The wrist axes are mutually perpendicular and the middle joint's axis is parallel to the previous link").

---

## 2.5 Motion Control — Detailed Comparison

### PTP (Point-to-Point) Motion
- **Endpoints only** are defined.
- Speed is **not controlled** between points (only at endpoints).
- Used for: **spot welding, palletizing, loading/unloading**.

### CP (Continuous Path) Motion
- **Both endpoints and intermediate path** are defined.
- Path and speed are both **controlled**.
- Used for: **arc welding, painting, sealing, glue dispensing**.

### Parameter Difference Table

| Parameter | PTP | CP |
|---|---|---|
| Endpoints defined | Yes | Yes |
| Path defined | No | Yes |
| Speed controlled | At endpoints | Throughout path |
| Cycle time | Faster | Slower |
| Memory needed | Less | More |
| Typical use | Pick-and-place | Spray painting |

### Joint-Interpolated vs Straight-Line Motion
- **Joint-Interpolated:** Each joint moves independently — fastest but path is curved.
- **Straight-Line (Linear):** End-effector moves in a true straight line — slower but precise.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Degrees of Freedom for a 6-DOF Robot

Let us derive the total DOF of a standard articulated robot (RRR + spherical wrist).

### Step 1 — Identify Each Joint

A typical 6-DOF articulated robot has the following joint sequence:

- Joint 1 — Base rotation (Revolute) → 1 DOF
- Joint 2 — Shoulder pitch (Revolute) → 1 DOF
- Joint 3 — Elbow pitch (Revolute) → 1 DOF
- Joint 4 — Wrist bend (Revolute) → 1 DOF
- Joint 5 — Wrist twist (Revolute) → 1 DOF
- Joint 6 — Wrist swivel (Revolute) → 1 DOF

### Step 2 — Apply the DOF Summation Formula

$$DOF = \sum_{i=1}^{6} f_i = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1$$

$$DOF = 6$$

### Step 3 — Interpret the Result

The robot has **6 independent DOF**, meaning it can:
- Position its end-effector at any $(x, y, z)$ location in space (3 DOF for position).
- Orient the end-effector at any roll-pitch-yaw combination (3 DOF for orientation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 6-DOF robot is the minimum required to achieve arbitrary position and orientation in 3D space. Adding a 7th DOF creates **kinematic redundancy** — useful for avoiding obstacles or singularities.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Grübler's Equation for a 4-Bar Planar Linkage

Consider a planar **four-bar linkage** as a simplified robotic finger.

### Step 1 — Identify Parameters

- Number of links (including ground): $n = 4$
- Number of revolute joints (each = 1 DOF lower pair): $J_1 = 4$
- Number of higher-pair joints: $J_2 = 0$

### Step 2 — Apply the Planar Grübler Equation

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2J_1 - J_2$$

$$M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0$$

$$M = 9 - 8$$

$$M = 1$$

### Step 3 — Interpret

A four-bar linkage has **1 DOF** — driving one link causes a deterministic motion of all others. This confirms the formula's correctness.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Resolution of a Robot Joint

Suppose a robot joint has a **range of motion** of $180°$ and uses a **12-bit encoder**.

### Step 1 — State the Resolution Formula

$$Resolution = \frac{Range}{2^n}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$Resolution = \frac{180°}{2^{12}}$$

$$Resolution = \frac{180°}{4096}$$

$$Resolution = 0.0439°$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Microradians

$$0.0439° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = 7.66 \times 10^{-4}\ rad$$

$$= 766\ \mu rad$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The smallest detectable angular movement of this joint is approximately **44 thousandths of a degree** — finer than the human eye can perceive.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Cylindrical Robot Work Envelope Volume

A cylindrical robot has:
- Radial reach: from $R_{min} = 0.5\ m$ to $R_{max} = 2\ m$
- Vertical stroke: $H = 3\ m$
- Base rotation: $360°$ (full circle)

### Step 1 — State the Volume Formula

$$V = \pi \left(R_{\max}^2 - R_{\min}^2\right) \cdot H$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$V = \pi \left(2^2 - 0.5^2\right) \cdot 3$$

$$V = \pi (4 - 0.25) \cdot 3$$

$$V = \pi \cdot 3.75 \cdot 3$$

$$V = 35.34\ m^3$$

### Step 3 — Final Answer

The cylindrical robot can access a **working volume of 35.34 cubic meters**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Robot Selection Criteria

The following matrix helps select the appropriate robot for an industrial application:

| Criterion | Cartesian | Cylindrical | Spherical | Articulated | SCARA | Parallel |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reach | Excellent | Good | Good | Excellent | Medium | Limited |
| Payload | High | High | Very High | Medium-High | Low-Medium | Low |
| Speed | Low | Medium | Medium | Medium | High | Very High |
| Accuracy | Very High | High | Medium | Medium | High | Very High |
| Repeatability | High | High | Medium | Medium | Very High | Very High |
| Floor Space | Large | Medium | Medium | Small | Small | Very Small |
| Cost | Medium | Medium | Medium-High | High | Medium | High |
| Best For | CNC, Assembly | Loading | Forging | Welding, Painting | PCB | Packaging |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Types for Robotic Systems

| Sensor | Measures | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Potentiometer** | Position (analog) | Joint angle sensing |
| **Encoder (Optical)** | Position (digital) | High-precision feedback |
| **Resolver** | Angular position | Harsh environments |
| **Tachogenerator** | Velocity | Motor speed control |
| **Strain Gauge** | Force / Torque | Force feedback, assembly |
| **LVDT** | Linear displacement | Hydraulic cylinder position |
| **Vision Camera** | 2D/3D images | Inspection, recognition |
| **Ultrasonic** | Distance | Obstacle detection |
| **Inertial (IMU)** | Acceleration, orientation | Mobile robots |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always mention the **closed-loop feedback path** when describing a robotic control system — sensors feed actual position back to the controller, which compares it with the commanded position (error signal) and corrects actuator output.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block Architecture of a Robotic System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        A1["Operator / Programmer"]
        A2["Teaching Pendant"]
        A3["Offline PC Interface"]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL SUBSYSTEM"]
        B1["Central Controller (CPU)"]
        B2["Memory Unit"]
        B3["Servo Amplifiers"]
        B4["Motion Planning Algorithm"]
    end

    subgraph DRIVE["DRIVE SUBSYSTEM"]
        C1["Power Supply"]
        C2["Actuator: Electric Motor"]
        C3["Actuator: Hydraulic Cylinder"]
        C4["Actuator: Pneumatic Piston"]
    end

    subgraph MANIPULATOR["MANIPULATOR SUBSYSTEM"]
        D1["Base Link"]
        D2["Joint 1: Revolute"]
        D3["Link 2"]
        D4["Joint 2: Revolute"]
        D5["Link 3"]
        D6["Joint 3: Prismatic"]
        D7["End Effector / Gripper"]
    end

    subgraph FEEDBACK["FEEDBACK SUBSYSTEM"]
        E1["Position Encoders"]
        E2["Velocity Sensors"]
        E3["Force / Torque Sensors"]
        E4["Vision System"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 <--> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D2
    C4 --> D6
    D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> D5 --> D6 --> D7
    E1 --> B1
    E2 --> B1
    E3 --> B1
    E4 --> B1

    style CONTROL fill:#ffe6e6
    style MANIPULATOR fill:#e6f3ff
    style FEEDBACK fill:#e6ffe6
    style DRIVE fill:#fff4e6
    style INPUT fill:#f0e6ff
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Classification Flowchart of Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT["Industrial Robots"]

    ROOT --> A["By Coordinate System"]
    ROOT --> B["By Drive System"]
    ROOT --> C["By Application"]
    ROOT --> D["By Generation"]
    ROOT --> E["By Control Method"]

    A --> A1["Cartesian PPP"]
    A --> A2["Cylindrical RPP"]
    A --> A3["Spherical RRP"]
    A --> A4["Articulated RRR"]
    A --> A5["SCARA RRP"]
    A --> A6["Parallel Delta"]

    B --> B1["Hydraulic"]
    B --> B2["Pneumatic"]
    B --> B3["Electric Servo"]

    C --> C1["Assembly Robots"]
    C --> C2["Welding Robots"]
    C --> C3["Painting Robots"]
    C --> C4["Material Handling Robots"]

    D --> D1["Playback Gen 1"]
    D --> D2["Sensory Gen 2"]
    D --> D3["Intelligent Gen 3"]

    E --> E1["PTP Control"]
    E --> E2["CP Control"]
    E --> E3["Servo Controlled"]
    E --> E4["Non Servo Controlled"]

    style ROOT fill:#ffd700
    style A fill:#e6f3ff
    style B fill:#fff4e6
    style C fill:#e6ffe6
    style D fill:#f0e6ff
    style E fill:#ffe6e6
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Anatomy of an Articulated Robot (6 DOF)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BASE["BASE FRAME<br/>Ground Mounted"]
    J1["JOINT 1<br/>Revolute Waist Rotation"]
    L1["LINK 1<br/>Shoulder Housing"]
    J2["JOINT 2<br/>Revolute Shoulder"]
    L2["LINK 2<br/>Upper Arm"]
    J3["JOINT 3<br/>Revolute Elbow"]
    L3["LINK 3<br/>Forearm"]
    J4["JOINT 4<br/>Revolute Wrist Bend"]
    L4["LINK 4<br/>Wrist Housing"]
    J5["JOINT 5<br/>Revolute Wrist Twist"]
    J6["JOINT 6<br/>Revolute Wrist Swivel"]
    EE["END EFFECTOR<br/>Gripper or Tool"]

    BASE --> J1 --> L1 --> J2 --> L2 --> J3 --> L3 --> J4 --> L4 --> J5 --> J6 --> EE

    style BASE fill:#d3d3d3
    style J1 fill:#ffcccb
    style J2 fill:#ffcccb
    style J3 fill:#ffcccb
    style J4 fill:#add8e6
    style J5 fill:#add8e6
    style J6 fill:#add8e6
    style EE fill:#90ee90
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Closed-Loop Robotic Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT["Reference Input<br/>Desired Position"]
    COMPARATOR["Comparator"]
    ERROR["Error Signal e t"]
    CONTROLLER["Controller<br/>PID Algorithm"]
    AMP["Servo Amplifier"]
    MOTOR["Actuator Motor"]
    ROBOT["Robot Joint"]
    OUTPUT["Actual Output Position"]
    SENSOR["Feedback Sensor<br/>Encoder"]
    FEEDBACK_PATH["Feedback Path"]

    INPUT --> COMPARATOR
    SENSOR --> COMPARATOR
    COMPARATOR --> ERROR --> CONTROLLER --> AMP --> MOTOR --> ROBOT --> OUTPUT
    OUTPUT --> SENSOR
    SENSOR --> FEEDBACK_PATH --> COMPARATOR

    style CONTROLLER fill:#ffe6e6
    style SENSOR fill:#e6ffe6
    style COMPARATOR fill:#fff4e6
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Motion Types Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MOT["Robot Motion Control"]

    MOT --> P["PTP Point to Point"]
    MOT --> CP["CP Continuous Path"]

    P --> P1["Only endpoints defined"]
    P --> P2["Path between uncontrolled"]
    P --> P3["Spot Welding"]
    P --> P4["Pick and Place"]
    P --> P5["High Speed"]
    P --> P6["Low Memory"]

    CP --> C1["Path explicitly defined"]
    CP --> C2["Multiple intermediate points"]
    CP --> C3["Arc Welding"]
    CP --> C4["Spray Painting"]
    CP --> C5["Controlled Speed"]
    CP --> C6["High Memory Need"]

    style P fill:#e6f3ff
    style CP fill:#ffe6e6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. State Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The Three Laws of Robotics were formulated by **Isaac Asimov** in 1942. In order of priority they are:

1. **First Law:** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
2. **Second Law:** A robot must obey orders given by authorized humans, except where such orders conflict with the First Law.
3. **Third Law:** A robot must protect its own existence, as long as this does not conflict with the First or Second Law.

A **Zeroth Law** (added later) states: *A robot may not harm humanity as a whole, or allow humanity to come to harm.* These laws serve as an ethical framework for autonomous robotic systems.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between PTP and CP motion control. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PTP (Point-to-Point) | CP (Continuous Path) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Movement between two taught points only | Movement along a defined continuous trajectory |
| Path Control | Path between points not controlled | Path fully controlled |
| Speed Control | Only at endpoints | Throughout the trajectory |
| Memory Required | Low | High |
| Applications | Pick-and-place, spot welding | Arc welding, spray painting |
| Cycle Time | Fast | Slower |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex |

PTP is suitable when the **start and end positions matter** but the intermediate path is irrelevant, whereas CP is essential when the **entire path shape matters**, such as in painting curved surfaces.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **DO NOT** confuse *Degrees of Freedom* with *Number of Axes* — they are the same numerically but conceptually refer to independent motions.
> 2. **DO NOT** forget to mention the **wrist center** when describing wrist configurations.
> 3. **DO NOT** misclassify SCARA as cylindrical — SCARA has 2 revolute joints followed by a vertical prismatic.
> 4. **Always** draw a labeled diagram — even for 14-mark theory questions, a sketch can fetch **1–2 grace marks**.
