> 5. **Always** state the **units** for resolution and accuracy calculations.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) Explain the basic structure of a robot with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each subsystem.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The basic structure of an industrial robot consists of **four major subsystems**:

**1. Manipulator (Mechanical Unit):**
This is the physical body of the robot. It consists of a sequence of rigid links connected by joints. The manipulator includes:
- **Base:** Provides mechanical support and houses the first joint.
- **Links:** Rigid structural members.
- **Joints:** Either revolute (rotational) or prismatic (linear).
- **End-effector:** The tool/gripper attached to the wrist.

**2. Controller:**
The "brain" of the robot. It stores the program, processes sensor inputs, computes joint trajectories using inverse kinematics, and sends command signals to servo amplifiers. Modern controllers use **32-bit microprocessors** and support multiple programming languages.

**3. Power Supply / Drive System:**
Provides regulated energy to actuators. Common types:
- **Electric:** Servo motors or stepper motors (most common).
- **Hydraulic:** For high-payload applications (>500 kg).
- **Pneumatic:** For simple two-position pick-and-place.

**4. Sensors:**
Provide feedback to the controller for closed-loop operation. Examples: optical encoders, potentiometers, tachometers, vision systems, force/torque sensors.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 — Robot System Architecture)

---

**(b) Classify robots based on coordinate system. Explain any two with neat sketches.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Industrial robots are classified into **six major types** based on the sequence of joint types used to position the end-effector.

The six types are:
1. **Cartesian (PPP):** All three joints are prismatic along X, Y, Z.
2. **Cylindrical (RPP):** Base revolute + two prismatic (vertical and radial).
3. **Spherical / Polar (RRP):** Two revolute joints + one prismatic.
4. **Articulated / Revolute (RRR):** Three revolute joints.
5. **SCARA (RRP):** Two revolute in horizontal plane + one vertical prismatic.
6. **Parallel (Delta):** Multiple arms meet at single end-effector.

### Explanation 1 — Cartesian Robot (PPP)

**Construction:** Three prismatic joints arranged orthogonally along X, Y, Z axes.
**Work Envelope:** Rectangular cuboid.
**Advantages:**
- High positional accuracy.
- Simple kinematics — easy to program.
- Large workspace.
**Disadvantages:**
- Requires large floor space.
- Limited reach to hidden areas.
**Applications:** CNC machine loading, 3D printing, assembly.

### Explanation 2 — Articulated Robot (RRR)

**Construction:** Three revolute joints — base rotation, shoulder pivot, elbow joint.
**Work Envelope:** Roughly spherical with inner cavity.
**Advantages:**
- Maximum flexibility — can reach around obstacles.
- Largest reachable workspace for given arm length.
- Ideal for complex welding/painting paths.
**Disadvantages:**
- Complex kinematics — requires inverse kinematics solver.
- Lower accuracy compared to Cartesian.
**Applications:** Automotive welding, spray painting, material handling.

> **KTU Tip:** Always include a labeled sketch of each robot type for full marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a) Explain the law of robotics. Discuss their relevance in modern AI-driven robotic systems.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Introduction

Issac Asimov formulated the **Three Laws of Robotics** in his 1942 short story *"Runaround"*. These were the first formalized ethical rules for autonomous machines and have since influenced all discussions on robot ethics.

### The Three Laws (in priority order)

**Law 1 — Non-Injury Principle:**
A robot may not injure a human being, or through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
- *Engineering Translation:* Hard-wired safety interlocks, force-torque limits, emergency stop, collision detection.

**Law 2 — Obedience Principle:**
A robot must obey orders from authorized humans, except where such orders conflict with Law 1.
- *Engineering Translation:* Authentication of user commands, role-based access control.

**Law 3 — Self-Preservation Principle:**
A robot must protect its own existence, unless it conflicts with Laws 1 or 2.
- *Engineering Translation:* Built-in diagnostics, battery management, fault recovery routines.

**Zeroth Law (1985 Addition):**
A robot may not harm humanity, or through inaction, allow humanity to come to harm.
- *Engineering Translation:* Swarm-level AI constraints, collective decision algorithms.

### Relevance in Modern AI-Driven Systems

1. **Autonomous Vehicles:** Law 1 directly applies to pedestrian safety algorithms; dilemma scenarios (trolley problem) are now resolved using Asimov-inspired frameworks.
2. **Medical Robots (Da Vinci, surgical bots):** Law 1 enforces tissue-damage limits; Law 2 governs surgeon command authority.
3. **Industrial Cobots:** Law 3 is critical for safe stop-and-resume behavior in human-shared workspaces.
4. **AI Ethics Committees:** Modern frameworks like the **IEEE Ethically Aligned Design** and **EU AI Act** borrow directly from Asimov's hierarchy.
5. **Military Robots:** The Zeroth Law is invoked in rules of engagement algorithms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critique:** Modern researchers note that the laws are **too simplistic** for true AGI (Artificial General Intelligence) — they assume binary harm and a single-agent scenario. Advanced extensions include **Murphy and Woods' "Better Laws"** and **Arkin's Ethical Governor** for autonomous weapons.

---

**(b) What is meant by wrist configuration? Explain the different wrist joints and their notations with diagrams.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Definition of Wrist

The **wrist** is the terminal segment of the robotic manipulator that connects the arm to the end-effector. Its primary function is to **orient** the end-effector without changing the position of the wrist center.

### Number of DOF

A standard industrial robot has **3 DOF in the wrist**, providing **roll, pitch, and yaw** orientation. These are typically achieved using three rotational joints whose axes **intersect at a single point** called the **wrist center**.

### Wrist Joint Nomenclature

Each wrist joint is described by the motion it performs:

| Joint | Motion | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **B (Bend)** | Pitch | Rotation up/down |
| **T (Twist)** | Yaw | Rotation about the forearm axis |
| **S (Swivel)** | Roll | Rotation about the tool axis |
| **V (Volute)** | Roll + offset | Combined rotation with translation |

### Standard Wrist Configurations

**1. B-T-S Configuration (Most Common):**
The first wrist joint bends (pitch), the second twists (yaw), and the third swivels (roll). All three axes are mutually perpendicular. Used in most general-purpose industrial robots (e.g., ABB IRB series).

**2. B-S-T Configuration:**
The first two joints are parallel (bend + swivel) and the third twists. This arrangement avoids the **singularity** that occurs in B-T-S when joints 4 and 6 align.

**3. B-V-S Configuration:**
The middle joint has a combined roll-translation motion, allowing the end-effector to wrap around obstacles. Used in tight-space assembly operations.

**4. Universal-Universal (U-U):**
Two universal joints in series, providing 2 DOF with compact construction. Less common in industry but found in some aerospace applications.

### Spherical Wrist Condition

For a wrist to be classified as **spherical**, all three joint axes must intersect at a single point. This simplifies the **inverse kinematics** dramatically because the position of the end-effector depends only on the first three joints (arm), while the orientation depends only on the last three joints (wrist) — this is called the **kinematic decoupling** property.

### Diagram

(Refer to Section 4.3 — the joints J4, J5, J6 in the articulated robot diagram represent a typical B-T-S wrist.)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**

### Core Definitions
- ✅ **Robot (ISO):** Automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator in **3 or more axes**.
- ✅ **Asimov's 3 Laws:** Non-injury → Obedience → Self-preservation (priority order).
- ✅ **DOF:** Number of independent movements in space (typical industrial robot = **6**).
- ✅ **Wrist Center:** Common intersection point of last 3 joint axes.

### Robot Classification
- ✅ Six types: **Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical (RRP), Articulated (RRR), SCARA, Parallel**.
- ✅ Three generations: **Playback, Sensory, Intelligent**.
- ✅ Three drives: **Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Electric**.

### Joints & Motion
- ✅ Two main joint types in industry: **Revolute (R)** and **Prismatic (P)**.
- ✅ Work envelope shape by robot type: **cuboid, cylinder, sphere, irregular sphere**.

### Motion Control
- ✅ **PTP:** Endpoints only → spot welding, pick-and-place.
- ✅ **CP:** Full path controlled → arc welding, painting.

### Key Formulas to Memorize
- ✅ $DOF = \sum f_i$
- ✅ Grübler planar: $M = 3(n-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$
- ✅ Resolution = $\frac{Range}{2^n}$
- ✅ Cylindrical volume: $V = \pi (R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2) \cdot H$

### Examiner's Golden Rules
- ✅ Always **draw a labeled diagram**.
- ✅ Always **state units** for numerical answers.
- ✅ Use **terminology from the KTU syllabus** (e.g., "manipulator", "end-effector", "wrist center").
- ✅ Mention **real-world applications** for every classification.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT721 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS & AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Roll, Pitch, Yaw, Drives, Hydraulic Motors, DC Motor, Stepper Motor, Power Transmission Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Robot Manipulator Orientation: Roll, Pitch, and Yaw

In the context of industrial robotics, the spatial orientation of a robot's end-effector (gripper/tool) or any rigid body in 3D space is universally described using three rotational degrees of freedom about a fixed Cartesian coordinate frame, referred to as **Roll**, **Pitch**, and **Yaw**. These three rotations are collectively termed the **Euler Angle Representation** of orientation and form the backbone of the **RPY (Roll-Pitch-Yaw) Convention**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition – Roll-Pitch-Yaw (RPY):**
> Roll, Pitch, and Yaw are the three principal angular displacements that describe the orientation of a robot end-effector (or any rigid body) with respect to a fixed reference frame, using right-hand rule rotations about the body-fixed X, Y, and Z axes respectively.

### Detailed Definitions

| Motion | Axis of Rotation | Plane of Movement | Direction (Right-Hand Rule) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Roll (φ)** | **X-axis (Longitudinal)** | Y-Z Plane (Side-to-Side) | Rotation of body about its forward/back axis |
| **Pitch (θ)** | **Y-axis (Transverse/Lateral)** | X-Z Plane (Up-Down) | Rotation of body about its side axis |
| **Yaw (ψ)** | **Z-axis (Vertical)** | X-Y Plane (Left-Right) | Rotation of body about its vertical axis |

### Conceptual Analogy – "The Pilot's Head and the Ship's Bow"

Imagine you are a fighter-jet pilot sitting in the cockpit:
- **Roll** is the motion when you tilt your head sideways (tilt your left ear toward your left shoulder). Your nose still points forward, but the wings of the jet tilt up and down.
- **Pitch** is the motion when you nod your head up and down (like saying "yes"). The nose of the jet moves up toward the sky or down toward the ground.
- **Yaw** is the motion when you shake your head left and right (like saying "no"). The nose of the jet swings horizontally left and right.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For a 6-DOF (Six Degrees of Freedom) industrial robot, **three DOFs are translational (X, Y, Z)** and **three DOFs are rotational (Roll, Pitch, Yaw)**. The RPY angles are the rotational part of the **forward kinematics** problem.

---

## 1.2 Robot Actuators and Drives – A Formal Introduction

An **actuator** is the "muscle" of a robot – the energy-conversion device that produces mechanical motion (linear or rotary) in response to an electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic control signal. The term **drive** refers to the complete power-transmission package, which includes the actuator, power supply, feedback sensors, and control electronics.

### Classification of Robot Drives (KTU 2024 Module 1)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition – Drive System:**
> A robot drive system is an integrated mechatronic package that converts input energy (electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic) into precisely controlled mechanical motion at the robot joints, enabling positioning and orientation of the end-effector.

**1. Electric Drives**
- **DC Motors** (Brushed, Brushless)
- **AC Motors** (Induction, Synchronous, Servo)
- **Stepper Motors** (Variable Reluctance, Permanent Magnet, Hybrid)

**2. Hydraulic Drives**
- Hydraulic Cylinders (Linear Actuators)
- **Hydraulic Motors** (Rotary Actuators – Gear, Vane, Piston)

**3. Pneumatic Drives**
- Air cylinders, air motors
- Used for simple ON/OFF pick-and-place applications

### Conceptual Analogy – "The Three Engines of a Robot"

Think of a robot as a human body:
- **Electric drives** are like the **nervous system** – fast, precise, clean, suitable for fine finger movements (small parts assembly).
- **Hydraulic drives** are like the **heavy muscles of an elephant** – extremely powerful, used to lift car chassis on assembly lines.
- **Pneumatic drives** are like the **stiffness of a hammer** – quick, forceful, but not very precise; used for simple clamping or blowing operations.

---

## 1.3 Hydraulic Motors – The Robotic "Heavy Lifter"

A **hydraulic motor** is a rotary mechanical actuator that converts hydraulic energy (pressurized fluid) into mechanical rotational energy (torque and angular velocity). It is the hydraulic counterpart of an electric motor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition – Hydraulic Motor:**
> A hydraulic motor is a rotary actuator that converts the pressure energy of a hydraulic fluid into continuous rotary motion, delivering high torque at relatively low rotational speeds, and is used in robots requiring high payload capacity.

**Common Types of Hydraulic Motors:**
- **Gear Motors** (External and Internal Gear) – Simple, low cost, used for medium pressure.
- **Vane Motors** – Smooth operation, used for medium-speed applications.
- **Piston Motors** (Axial and Radial) – Highest efficiency, used in high-performance industrial robots.

### Conceptual Analogy – "The Water Wheel of the Robot"

Imagine a wooden water wheel in a stream. As pressurized water (hydraulic fluid) flows over the wheel's buckets, the wheel rotates. The faster and harder the water flows, the more powerful the rotation. A hydraulic motor works the same way – pressurized oil entering the motor's chambers pushes against vanes, gears, or pistons, causing the output shaft to rotate. The more oil flow (Q) and pressure (P), the more rotational power is delivered.

---

## 1.4 DC Motor – The Workhorse of Educational and Light-Payload Robots

A **DC (Direct Current) Motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that converts DC electrical energy into mechanical rotational energy based on the principle of the Lorentz force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition – DC Motor:**
> A DC motor is a rotary electrical machine that operates on direct current, producing torque proportional to the armature current and flux per pole, used widely in robot joint actuation, conveyor systems, and servo-positioning applications.

**Main Parts of a DC Motor:**
- **Stator (Field System):** Yoke, Field Poles, Field Windings (produce magnetic flux Φ).
- **Rotor (Armature):** Armature core, Armature windings, Commutator, Brushes.
- **Auxiliary:** Bearings, Shaft, Cooling fan, Brush gear.

**Types of DC Motors Used in Robotics:**
- **DC Servo Motor** – Used in precise position control.
- **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor** – High efficiency, long life, used in modern collaborative robots.
- **DC Geared Motor** – Combines DC motor with gearbox for high torque at low speed.

### Conceptual Analogy – "The Electric Spinning Top"

Imagine holding a battery connected to a coil of wire sitting between two magnets. When current flows through the coil, one side is pushed by the north pole and pulled by the south pole, causing the coil to spin. The commutator (a mechanical switch) keeps flipping the current direction so the coil spins continuously. The stronger the battery (voltage) and the heavier the load (torque), the more current flows, and the faster/heavier the rotation.

---

## 1.5 Stepper Motor – The Robotic "Digital Pointer"

A **stepper motor** (also called a **stepping motor**) is a brushless, synchronous electric motor that divides a full rotation into a large number of discrete, equally spaced angular steps. Each electrical pulse applied to the motor causes the shaft to rotate by a fixed angular increment called the **step angle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition – Stepper Motor:**
> A stepper motor is a special type of synchronous motor in which the rotor moves in discrete angular steps for each electrical input pulse, providing open-loop position control without the need for feedback encoders.

**Types of Stepper Motors:**
- **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor** – Rotor aligns to position of minimum reluctance.
- **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor** – Rotor has permanent magnets; detent torque at rest.
- **Hybrid Stepper Motor** – Combines features of VR and PM, gives the smallest step angles (typically 0.9° to 1.8°).

### Conceptual Analogy – "The Second Hand of a Clock"

A quartz clock's second hand doesn't sweep continuously; it ticks forward in tiny discrete steps (60 steps per minute = 6° per step). A stepper motor behaves the same way: it doesn't spin freely like a fan. Instead, it rotates in small, exact, repeatable "ticks." If you send 100 pulses, it moves exactly 100 steps, making it perfect for 3D printers, CNC machines, and pick-and-place robots where exact positioning is critical.

---

## 1.6 Power Transmission Systems – The Robot's "Belt and Gear Train"

A **Power Transmission System** is a mechanical arrangement that transmits rotational power (torque × angular velocity) from the actuator's output shaft to the robot joint at the required speed and torque. The transmission modifies the **speed-torque characteristics** of the prime mover (motor) to suit the load.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition – Power Transmission System:**
> A power transmission system in a robot is a set of mechanical elements (gears, belts, chains, lead screws) that transfer and modify the mechanical power from the actuator to the load, allowing trade-off between speed and torque.

**Common Types of Power Transmission Systems in Robotics:**

| Type | Mechanism | Typical Application in Robots |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gear Drive** | Meshing toothed wheels | Joint reducers, harmonic drives in robot wrists |
| **Belt Drive** | Pulleys + flexible belt | Light-duty conveyors, low-cost robotic arms |
| **Chain Drive** | Sprockets + roller chain | Heavy-duty joint drives, palletizing robots |
| **Lead Screw / Ball Screw** | Helical threads | Linear actuators in Cartesian robots |
| **Harmonic Drive** | Wave generator + flexspline | High-ratio reducers in precision robots |
| **Cable / Tendon Drive** | Flexible cables over pulleys | Anthropomorphic robots, surgical robots |

### Conceptual Analogy – "The Bicycle Gear System"

When you ride a bicycle uphill, you shift to a low gear: the chain wheel is small, the rear sprocket is large, the pedals feel easier to push, but the wheel turns slowly. This is exactly what a power transmission system does in a robot: it trades rotational **speed** for **torque** (or vice versa), so the motor can produce enough force to move the robot's joints despite gravity and payload.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Roll-Pitch-Yaw: Mathematical Foundation

The orientation of a rigid body in 3D space can be described by a **3×3 Rotation Matrix (R)** built by composing three elementary rotations about the principal axes. The order of multiplication matters (rotations are **non-commutative**).

The standard Z-Y-X (Yaw-Pitch-Roll) rotation matrix is:

$$R = R_z(\psi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_x(\phi)$$

Where:
- $R_z(\psi)$ = Yaw rotation about Z-axis by angle $\psi$
- $R_y(\theta)$ = Pitch rotation about Y-axis by angle $\theta$
- $R_x(\phi)$ = Roll rotation about X-axis by angle $\phi$

The complete composite rotation matrix is:

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} c\psi c\theta & c\psi s\theta s\phi - s\psi c\phi & c\psi s\theta c\phi + s\psi s\phi \\ s\psi c\theta & s\psi s\theta s\phi + c\psi c\phi & s\psi s\theta c\phi - c\psi s\phi \\ -s\theta & c\theta s\phi & c\theta c\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

Where $c\alpha = \cos\alpha$ and $s\alpha = \sin\alpha$.

### Step-by-Step Logic – Why These Three Rotations?

**Step 1:** **Roll (φ)** about X-axis – tilts the body sideways. Used in banking turns of aircraft, wrist rotation of robots.
**Step 2:** **Pitch (θ)** about Y-axis – raises/lowers the nose. Used in climbing dive angles, tool tilt in welding robots.
**Step 3:** **Yaw (ψ)** about Z-axis – swings the body horizontally. Used in steering, base rotation of a robot arm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** In **6-DOF industrial robots** (e.g., KUKA, FANUC, ABB), the first three joints (J1, J2, J3) provide the position of the wrist center, while the last three joints (J4, J5, J6) correspond to **Roll, Pitch, Yaw** of the end-effector.

---

## 2.2 DC Motor: Governing Equations and Torque-Speed Characteristics

### Working Principle (Lorentz Force Law)

When a current-carrying conductor of length $L$ is placed in a magnetic field of flux density $B$, it experiences a force:
$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L \quad \text{(Newtons)}$$

This force, acting on multiple conductors on the armature periphery, produces a torque that rotates the shaft.

### Key DC Motor Equations

**(a) Voltage Equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the armature circuit):**
$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

Where:
- $V$ = Supply voltage (Volts)
- $E_b$ = Back EMF (Volts)
- $I_a$ = Armature current (Amperes)
- $R_a$ = Armature resistance (Ohms)

**(b) Back EMF Equation (Faraday's Law of Induction):**
$$E_b = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (Weber)
- $N$ = Speed of armature (RPM)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths in the armature

**(c) Torque Equation:**
$$T = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a}{2\pi \cdot A}$$

**(d) Shaft Torque (Mechanical Output):**
$$T_{sh} = \frac{P_{out}}{\omega} = \frac{P_{out} \cdot 60}{2\pi N}$$

**(e) Speed Equation (Rearranged from Voltage Equation):**
$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{E_b / N} = \frac{60 \cdot A \cdot (V - I_a R_a)}{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept – Back EMF:** As the armature spins, the conductors cut magnetic flux lines, generating a voltage **opposing** the applied voltage. This back EMF limits the armature current and hence controls the speed. This is the fundamental reason a DC motor is **self-regulating** – if the load increases, the motor slows, back EMF drops, current rises, and torque increases to handle the new load.

---

## 2.3 Stepper Motor: Step Angle and Resolution

### Step Angle Derivation

For a stepper motor, the step angle is the angular displacement of the rotor for one input pulse. It is determined by the number of stator teeth and rotor teeth:

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r / (\text{GCD of teeth})} \quad \text{or simply} \quad \beta = \frac{360°}{\text{Steps per revolution}}$$

For a standard **hybrid stepper motor** with $N_s$ stator teeth and $N_r$ rotor teeth, the step angle is:

$$\beta = \frac{360° \cdot (N_s - N_r)}{N_s \cdot N_r}$$

**Resolutions of Common Stepper Motors:**

| Step Angle | Steps per Revolution | Common Application |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **0.9°** | 400 | High-precision CNC, 3D printers |
| **1.8°** | 200 | Most common – 3D printers, small robots |
| **3.75°** | 96 | Low-cost hobby projects |
| **7.5°** | 48 | Toys, simple positioning |
| **15°** | 24 | Very low resolution, legacy applications |

> [!NOTE]
> **Microstepping:** Modern drivers can subdivide each full step into 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, or 256 microsteps, achieving effective resolutions as fine as 0.007° per microstep. This is achieved by varying the current ratios between the two phase windings sinusoidally.

### Stepper Motor Characteristics

1. **Open-Loop Operation:** No encoder needed – the input pulse count directly determines position.
2. **Holding Torque:** Torque required to displace the rotor from its rest position; a key performance metric.
3. **Detent Torque:** Residual torque in PM-type steppers due to permanent magnets; holds position even when unpowered.
4. **Pull-Out Torque:** Maximum load torque at which the motor can start, accelerate, and run in synchronism.
5. **Pull-In Torque:** Maximum load torque the motor can start from rest in synchronism without losing steps.

---

## 2.4 Hydraulic Motors: Governing Equations

A hydraulic motor receives pressurized fluid (typically oil) from a pump and converts it to mechanical rotation. The fundamental relationship is:

$$P_{hydraulic} = P \cdot Q = T \cdot \omega$$

Where:
- $P$ = Pressure differential across motor (Pa or bar)
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate (m³/s or L/min)
- $T$ = Output torque (N·m)
- $\omega$ = Angular velocity (rad/s)

### Torque Developed by a Hydraulic Motor

The theoretical torque produced is:

$$T = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$$

Where:
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure drop across the motor (Pa)
- $V_D$ = Displacement volume per revolution (m³/rev)

### Speed of a Hydraulic Motor

$$N = \frac{Q}{V_D} \quad \text{(rev/s or RPM depending on units)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Application:** In the **Unimate** and **Cincinnati Milacron T3** hydraulic robots of the 1960s, hydraulic motors provided the high torque-density required to lift payloads of 100–500 kg. Modern **Bosch Rexroth** and **Parker Hannifin** hydraulic actuators are still used in heavy-payload robots (e.g., palletizing robots in automotive assembly).

---

## 2.5 Power Transmission Systems: Velocity Ratio and Mechanical Advantage

### Velocity Ratio (V.R.)

The velocity ratio is the ratio of the speed of the driving (input) member to the speed of the driven (output) member:

$$\text{Velocity Ratio (V.R.)} = \frac{N_{driver}}{N_{driven}} = \frac{T_{driven}}{T_{driver}} \quad \text{(ideal, lossless case)}$$

### Gear Drive Equations

For a pair of meshing gears:
$$N_1 \cdot T_1 = N_2 \cdot T_2 \quad \text{(power equality, ideal)}$$

$$\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1}$$

Where:
- $N_1, N_2$ = Speeds of pinion and gear (RPM)
- $T_1, T_2$ = Torques on pinion and gear shafts
- $D_1, D_2$ = Pitch diameters
- $Z_1, Z_2$ = Number of teeth

### Belt Drive Equations

For a flat/V-belt drive:
$$V = \pi \cdot D_1 \cdot N_1 = \pi \cdot D_2 \cdot N_2$$

$$\text{Velocity Ratio} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1}$$

### Harmonic Drive (Strain Wave Gear) – The Robot's Premium Reducer

A **harmonic drive** is a specialized compact gear mechanism that provides very high reduction ratios (from 30:1 up to 320:1) in a single stage. It consists of three components:
- **Wave Generator (WG):** Elliptical input cam with bearings.
- **Flexspline (FS):** Thin, flexible cup with external teeth.
- **Circular Spline (CS):** Rigid ring with internal teeth.

Reduction ratio: $i = \frac{Z_{FS} - Z_{CS}}{Z_{FS}}$ (e.g., 200 − 202 = −2 teeth difference, ratio = 100:1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Application:** Harmonic drives are standard in the wrist joints of **KUKA LBR iiwa**, **ABB IRB**, and **FANUC CRX** collaborative robots because they offer **zero backlash**, **high torque density**, and **compactness** – critical for precision assembly tasks.

---

| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | RPY Rotation Matrix | $R = R_z(\psi) R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi)$ | Composite 3×3 orientation matrix | dimensionless |
| 2 | DC Motor Voltage Eqn. | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Kirchhoff's law on armature circuit | Volts |
| 3 | DC Motor Back EMF | $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$ | EMF induced in rotating armature | Volts |
| 4 | DC Motor Torque | $T = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$ | Electromagnetic torque developed | N·m |

| 6 | Stepper Step Angle | $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{\text{Steps/rev}}$ | Angular displacement per pulse | degrees |
| 7 | Stepper Resolution | $R = \dfrac{1 \text{ rev}}{\beta / 360°}$ | Number of steps per revolution | steps/rev |
| 8 | Hydraulic Motor Torque | $T = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$ | Output torque from pressure | N·m |
| 9 | Hydraulic Motor Speed | $N = \dfrac{Q}{V_D}$ | RPM from flow rate | RPM |
| 10 | Hydraulic Power | $P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = T \cdot \omega$ | Energy balance | Watts |
| 11 | Gear Velocity Ratio | $VR = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_1} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ | Speed/torque trade-off | dimensionless |

| 13 | Harmonic Drive Ratio | $i = \dfrac{Z_{FS} - Z_{CS}}{Z_{FS}}$ | High-ratio strain wave gearing | dimensionless |
| 14 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{T_{out} \cdot \omega_{out}}{T_{in} \cdot \omega_{in}}$ | Power loss parameter | % |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Composite Roll-Pitch-Yaw Rotation Matrix

We derive the orientation matrix by composing three elementary rotations. The result is essential for the **forward kinematics** of any 6-DOF robot.

### Step 1: Elementary Rotation about X-axis (Roll, φ)

A rotation by angle $\phi$ about the X-axis transforms the basis vectors as follows:
- $\hat{x}' = \hat{x}$ (unchanged)
- $\hat{y}' = \cos\phi \cdot \hat{y} + \sin\phi \cdot \hat{z}$
- $\hat{z}' = -\sin\phi \cdot \hat{y} + \cos\phi \cdot \hat{z}$

This gives the elementary roll matrix:

$$R_x(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Elementary Rotation about Y-axis (Pitch, θ)

A rotation by angle $\theta$ about the Y-axis:
- $\hat{x}' = \cos\theta \cdot \hat{x} - \sin\theta \cdot \hat{z}$
- $\hat{y}' = \hat{y}$
- $\hat{z}' = \sin\theta \cdot \hat{x} + \cos\theta \cdot \hat{z}$

The elementary pitch matrix:

$$R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3: Elementary Rotation about Z-axis (Yaw, ψ)

A rotation by angle $\psi$ about the Z-axis:
- $\hat{x}' = \cos\psi \cdot \hat{x} + \sin\psi \cdot \hat{y}$
- $\hat{y}' = -\sin\psi \cdot \hat{x} + \cos\psi \cdot \hat{y}$
- $\hat{z}' = \hat{z}$

The elementary yaw matrix:

$$R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Composite RPY Matrix (Z-Y-X Order)

The composite rotation $R = R_z(\psi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_x(\phi)$ is computed as:

**First compute $M_1 = R_y(\theta) \cdot R_x(\phi)$:**

$$M_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta\sin\phi & \sin\theta\cos\phi \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta\sin\phi & \cos\theta\cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Now compute $R = R_z(\psi) \cdot M_1$:**

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta\sin\phi & \sin\theta\cos\phi \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta\sin\phi & \cos\theta\cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Resulting in the standard RPY orientation matrix:**

$$\boxed{R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi\cos\theta & \cos\psi\sin\theta\sin\phi - \sin\psi\cos\phi & \cos\psi\sin\theta\cos\phi + \sin\psi\sin\phi \\ \sin\psi\cos\theta & \sin\psi\sin\theta\sin\phi + \cos\psi\cos\phi & \sin\psi\sin\theta\cos\phi - \cos\psi\sin\phi \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta\sin\phi & \cos\theta\cos\phi \end{bmatrix}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of DC Motor Speed-Torque Relationship

We start with the **voltage equation** and **back EMF equation** and eliminate the armature current to obtain a linear relationship between speed ($N$) and torque ($T$).

### Step 1: Start with the Armature Voltage Equation

From Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the armature circuit:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

This says: the applied voltage $V$ is partly used to overcome the back EMF $E_b$ and partly dropped across the armature resistance $R_a$.

### Step 2: Substitute the Back EMF Expression

Recall that $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$. Substituting:

$$V = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} + I_a R_a$$

Solving for the armature current $I_a$:

$$I_a = \frac{V - \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}}{R_a} = \frac{60 A (V - \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A})}{60 A \cdot R_a} = \frac{60 A V - P \Phi Z N}{60 A R_a}$$

This simplifies to:

$$I_a = \frac{60 A V - P \Phi Z N}{60 A R_a}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Armature Current into the Torque Equation

The electromagnetic torque is $T = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$. Substituting $I_a$:

$$T = \frac{P \Phi Z}{2 \pi A} \cdot \left( \frac{60 A V - P \Phi Z N}{60 A R_a} \right)$$

$$T = \frac{P \Phi Z (60 A V - P \Phi Z N)}{2 \pi A \cdot 60 A R_a}$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Expression

Expanding the numerator:

$$T = \frac{60 A P \Phi Z V - (P \Phi Z)^2 N}{120 \pi A^2 R_a}$$

### Step 5: Solve for Speed N as a Function of Torque T

Rearranging to isolate $N$:

$$(P \Phi Z)^2 N = 60 A P \Phi Z V - 120 \pi A^2 R_a T$$

$$N = \frac{60 A P \Phi Z V - 120 \pi A^2 R_a T}{(P \Phi Z)^2}$$

### Step 6: Identify the No-Load and Full-Load Speeds

Setting $T = 0$ gives the **no-load speed** $N_0$:

$$N_0 = \frac{60 A V}{P \Phi Z}$$

Setting $N = 0$ gives the **stall torque** $T_{stall}$ (the starting torque at locked rotor):

$$T_{stall} = \frac{60 A P \Phi Z V}{120 \pi A^2 R_a} = \frac{P \Phi Z V}{2 \pi A R_a}$$

### Step 7: Final Linear Speed-Torque Characteristic

$$\boxed{N = N_0 \left( 1 - \frac{T}{T_{stall}} \right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is a **linear** relationship. The DC motor's natural characteristic is a straight line from $(0, N_0)$ to $(T_{stall}, 0)$ on the T-N plane. By adding external armature resistance or using feedback control, the slope can be changed to suit the load – this is the basis of the **Ward-Leonard speed control** system and modern **PWM-based servo drives**.

---

## 3.3 Stepper Motor: Step Angle Derivation for Variable Reluctance Motor

For a **Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor**, the step angle is determined by the geometry of stator and rotor teeth.

### Step 1: Identify the Stator and Rotor Tooth Counts

Let:
- $N_s$ = Number of stator teeth (poles)
- $N_r$ = Number of rotor teeth

### Step 2: Rotor Pitch Angle

The rotor tooth pitch (angular spacing between adjacent rotor teeth) is:

$$\theta_r = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

The stator tooth pitch is:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_s}$$

### Step 3: Step Angle from Tooth Mismatch

The step angle is determined by the **relative mismatch** between one stator pole pair and one rotor pole pair. For a VR stepper with two-phase excitation:

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{N_r} \cdot \left( \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} \right) = \frac{360° (N_s - N_r)}{N_s N_r}$$

### Step 4: Number of Steps per Revolution

The number of discrete steps per revolution is the reciprocal relationship:

$$\text{Steps/rev} = \frac{360°}{\beta} = \frac{N_s N_r}{(N_s - N_r)}$$

### Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A three-phase, 12-stator-tooth, 8-rotor-tooth variable reluctance stepper motor is given. Calculate the step angle and the number of steps per revolution.

**Solution:**

Given: $N_s = 12$, $N_r = 8$.

Step 1: Apply the step angle formula:

$$\beta = \frac{360° (N_s - N_r)}{N_s N_r} = \frac{360° \cdot (12 - 8)}{12 \cdot 8}$$

Step 2: Simplify the numerator and denominator:

$$\beta = \frac{360° \cdot 4}{96} = \frac{1440°}{96}$$

Step 3: Final calculation:

$$\beta = 15°$$

Step 4: Number of steps per revolution:

$$\text{Steps/rev} = \frac{360°}{15°} = 24 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**Answer:** The step angle is **15°** and the motor requires **24 pulses** for one complete revolution.

---

## 3.4 Hydraulic Motor: Torque and Power Calculation

**Problem:** A hydraulic motor has a displacement of $V_D = 100 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ and operates at a pressure difference of $\Delta P = 140 \text{ bar}$. The supply flow rate is $Q = 60 \text{ LPM}$ (litres per minute). Calculate:
1. The theoretical torque output
2. The rotational speed
3. The hydraulic power input
4. The mechanical power output (assuming 90% volumetric and 95% mechanical efficiency)

### Solution

**Given:**
- $V_D = 100 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$
- $\Delta P = 140 \text{ bar} = 140 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.4 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$
- $Q = 60 \text{ LPM} = \frac{60 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

**Step 1: Theoretical Torque**

Using $T = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$:

$$T = \frac{1.4 \times 10^7 \times 10^{-4}}{2\pi} = \frac{1400}{6.2832}$$

$$T = 222.8 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2: Rotational Speed**

Using $N = \dfrac{Q}{V_D}$:

$$N = \frac{10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}{10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}} = 10 \text{ rev/s} = 600 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3: Hydraulic Power Input**

$$P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q = 1.4 \times 10^7 \cdot 10^{-3} = 14000 \text{ W} = 14 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Mechanical Power Output**

Total efficiency: $\eta_{total} = \eta_{volumetric} \cdot \eta_{mechanical} = 0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.855$

$$P_{mech} = \eta_{total} \cdot P_{hyd} = 0.855 \times 14000 = 11970 \text{ W} \approx 11.97 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When pressure is given in **bar**, always convert to **Pa** by multiplying by $10^5$. When flow rate is given in **LPM**, divide by **60000** to get m³/s. Keep **3 significant figures** in your final answer unless told otherwise.

---

## 3.5 Power Transmission: Gear Train and Harmonic Drive Analysis

**Problem:** A robot joint is driven by a DC motor (3000 RPM, 2 N·m torque) through a two-stage gear reducer with ratios 5:1 and 4:1 (total 20:1), followed by a harmonic drive with reduction ratio 100:1. The overall efficiency of the gear train is 80%, and the harmonic drive is 90% efficient.
1. Calculate the output speed at the joint.
2. Calculate the output torque at the joint.
3. What is the overall transmission ratio?

### Solution

**Step 1: Overall Transmission Ratio**

$$i_{total} = 5 \times 4 \times 100 = 2000:1$$

**Step 2: Output Speed**

$$N_{out} = \frac{N_{in}}{i_{total}} = \frac{3000}{2000} = 1.5 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3: Output Torque**

First compute ideal torque amplification (no losses): $T_{ideal} = T_{in} \times i_{total} = 2 \times 2000 = 4000 \text{ N·m}$.

Apply overall efficiency $\eta_{overall} = 0.80 \times 0.90 = 0.72$:

$$T_{out} = T_{ideal} \times \eta_{overall} = 4000 \times 0.72 = 2880 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Verification by Power Conservation**

Input power: $P_{in} = T_{in} \cdot \omega_{in} = 2 \times \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 2 \times 314.16 = 628.3 \text{ W}$

Output power: $P_{out} = T_{out} \cdot \omega_{out} = 2880 \times \frac{2\pi \times 1.5}{60} = 2880 \times 0.1571 = 452.4 \text{ W}$

Efficiency check: $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{452.4}{628.3} = 0.720$ ✓ Matches 72%.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**Define Roll, Pitch, and Yaw in the context of robot manipulator orientation. State the corresponding axes of rotation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In robotics, Roll, Pitch, and Yaw are the three rotational degrees of freedom that completely describe the orientation of a robot's end-effector in 3D space. The definitions are:

- **Roll (φ):** Rotation of the end-effector about the **X-axis (longitudinal axis)** of the body-fixed frame. It produces a tilting motion from side to side, analogous to an aircraft banking.
- **Pitch (θ):** Rotation of the end-effector about the **Y-axis (lateral/transverse axis)**. It produces a tilting motion up and down, analogous to an aircraft climbing or diving.
- **Yaw (ψ):** Rotation of the end-effector about the **Z-axis (vertical axis)**. It produces a swinging motion left and right, analogous to an aircraft changing its heading.

Together, these three rotations form the **RPY (Roll-Pitch-Yaw) convention**, which is one of the standard methods (along with Euler angles and quaternions) for representing orientation in the **forward kinematics** of a 6-DOF robot.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**List any three differences between a DC motor and a Stepper motor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| S.No. | DC Motor | Stepper Motor |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Control Type** | Continuous rotation; speed is controlled by varying voltage. Used typically in closed-loop servo systems. | Discrete rotation in fixed angular steps; position is controlled by counting input pulses. Operates in open-loop. |
| **2. Feedback Required** | Requires position/speed feedback (encoder/tachometer) for precise control. | No feedback sensor is needed; position is determined by pulse count. |
| **3. Speed-Torque** | Produces high speed at low torque; requires gearbox for high-torque applications. | Operates best at low speeds but produces high holding torque inherently. |
| **4. Application** | Conveyor belts, robot wheels, fans, continuous-process machinery. | 3D printers, CNC machines, pick-and-place robots, camera lens focusing. |
| **5. Construction** | Has commutator and brushes (in brushed type) which wear out. | Brushless construction; no commutator; long service life. |
| **6. Holding at Position** | Cannot hold a position without external brake or active control. | Has inherent **holding torque** at standstill, even when unpowered. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a **DC motor**. Derive the **back EMF equation** and the **torque equation** of a DC motor.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 4-pole, 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.2 Ω and a field resistance of 110 Ω. The motor draws an armature current of 20 A at a certain load. Calculate:
1. The back EMF
2. The speed of the motor (assume flux per pole = 0.02 Wb, total armature conductors = 500, A = 2)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) Solution:**

A DC motor converts direct current electrical energy into mechanical rotational energy. Its construction has two main parts:

**Stator (Field System):**
- **Yoke** – The outer cylindrical frame; provides mechanical support and carries the magnetic flux.
- **Pole Cores and Pole Shoes** – Made of laminated silicon steel; concentrate the magnetic flux and support the field windings.
- **Field Windings** – Copper wire coils wound around the pole cores; when energized, produce the working magnetic flux Φ.
- **Brushes** – Carbon blocks that press against the commutator; supply DC current to the rotating armature.

**Rotor (Armature):**
- **Armature Core** – A laminated cylindrical iron body with slots on the outer periphery.
- **Armature Windings** – Insulated copper conductors placed in the slots; current flows through them and they experience a force.
- **Commutator** – A cylindrical assembly of copper segments insulated from each other; acts as a mechanical rectifier to keep the torque unidirectional.

**Working Principle:** When DC supply is given to the armature windings via the brushes and commutator, current-carrying conductors placed in a magnetic field experience a force $F = BIL$ (Lorentz force). This force produces a torque $T = F \times r$ that rotates the armature. The commutator reverses the current direction in each coil every half revolution, ensuring **unidirectional (continuous) rotation**.

**Back EMF Derivation:**

When the armature rotates, the conductors cut the magnetic flux lines. By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, an EMF is induced that **opposes** the applied voltage (Lenz's Law). This is called the **back EMF** or **counter EMF**.

For one conductor, the average EMF induced during one revolution is:
$$e = \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \frac{\Phi \cdot P \cdot N}{60} \text{ (Volts)}$$

Where $P$ is the number of poles and $N$ is the speed in RPM.

For $Z$ total conductors connected in $A$ parallel paths, the total back EMF is:
$$E_b = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$$

**Torque Equation Derivation:**

The force on a single conductor carrying current $I_a / A$ in a magnetic field of flux density $B = \Phi / A$ (pole area) is:
$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L = \frac{\Phi}{A} \cdot \frac{I_a}{A} \cdot L$$

Torque per conductor at radius $r$ (where $L \times r = \pi D L / 2$ for a cylindrical armature):
$$T_{per\ conductor} = F \cdot r$$

For $Z$ total conductors acting simultaneously:
$$T = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a}{2\pi \cdot A}$$

This is the **electromagnetic torque equation** of a DC motor.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 220$ V
- $P = 4$ poles
- $R_a = 0.2$ Ω
- $R_f = 110$ Ω (irrelevant for back EMF/speed since motor current is given)
- $I_a = 20$ A
- $\Phi = 0.02$ Wb
- $Z = 500$ conductors
- $A = P = 4$ (for wave-wound) or $A = 2$ (for lap-wound; assume lap as $A=2$ given)

**Step 1: Back EMF Calculation**

Using the voltage equation: $E_b = V - I_a R_a$

$$E_b = 220 - (20)(0.2) = 220 - 4 = 216 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Speed Calculation**

Using the back EMF formula: $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$

Solving for $N$:
$$N = \frac{60 \cdot A \cdot E_b}{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}$$

$$N = \frac{60 \times 2 \times 216}{4 \times 0.02 \times 500}$$

$$N = \frac{25920}{40} = 648 \text{ RPM}$$

**Final Answers:** Back EMF = **216 V**, Motor speed = **648 RPM**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **classification of robot drives** with a comparative analysis of **electric, hydraulic, and pneumatic drives**. State one industrial application for each type.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **variable reluctance stepper motor** has 8 stator poles and 6 rotor teeth. The motor is excited by a pulse train of 1200 pulses per minute. Calculate:
1. The step angle of the motor
2. The shaft speed in RPM
3. The number of pulses required to rotate the shaft by 90°

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) Solution:**

Robot drives are classified based on the energy source used to actuate the robot joints. The three main categories are:

**1. Electric Drives**

Electric drives convert electrical energy from batteries or mains supply into mechanical motion using electromagnetic principles.

- **Advantages:** High precision, easy controllability, fast response, clean operation (no oil leaks), low maintenance, compact size.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited torque density (less powerful than hydraulic for the same size), heat dissipation issues at high loads.
- **Types Used in Robotics:** DC servo motors, AC servo motors, stepper motors, brushless DC (BLDC) motors.
- **Example Industrial Application:** **SCARA robot pick-and-place machine** in a printed circuit board (PCB) assembly line uses electric servo drives to position surface-mount components with 0.01 mm accuracy.

**2. Hydraulic Drives**

Hydraulic drives use pressurized oil (typically at 70–200 bar) supplied by a central pump to actuate either linear cylinders or rotary motors.

- **Advantages:** Highest power-to-weight ratio, very high torque and force capacity, smooth operation, self-lubricating.
- **Disadvantages:** Expensive installation, requires pumps, valves, filters, hoses; risk of oil leakage; noisy; slower response than electric drives.
- **Example Industrial Application:** **Automotive body welding robots** (e.g., FANUC R-2000iB) use hydraulic actuators to lift and position car body panels weighing 100–500 kg with high stiffness.

**3. Pneumatic Drives**

Pneumatic drives use compressed air (typically at 4–8 bar) to actuate cylinders or air motors.

- **Advantages:** Cheapest drive system, very fast actuation, simple design, clean (no oil contamination), intrinsically safe in explosive environments.
- **Disadvantages:** Low force/torque output, compressibility of air makes precise position control difficult, noisy exhaust.
- **Example Industrial Application:** **Pneumatic pick-and-place mechanism** in food packaging industry, where simple ON/OFF actuation of grippers and diverters is needed at very high speed.

**Comparative Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Electric | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Power Source | Electricity | Pressurized oil | Compressed air |
| Pressure/Voltage | 12–480 V | 70–350 bar | 4–10 bar |
| Precision | Very High | Medium | Low |
| Payload | Low–Medium | Very High | Low |
| Cost | Medium | High | Low |
| Maintenance | Low | High (seals, filters) | Medium |
| Speed of Response | Very Fast | Medium | Fast |
| Cleanliness | Clean | Risk of oil leak | Clean |

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Given:**
- Stator poles: $N_s = 8$
- Rotor teeth: $N_r = 6$
- Pulse train frequency: $f = 1200$ pulses/min = 20 pulses/sec

**Step 1: Step Angle Calculation**

For a VR stepper motor, the step angle formula is:
$$\beta = \frac{360° (N_s - N_r)}{N_s \cdot N_r}$$

Substituting:
$$\beta = \frac{360° (8 - 6)}{8 \times 6} = \frac{360° \times 2}{48} = \frac{720°}{48} = 15°$$

**Step 2: Shaft Speed in RPM**

Number of steps per revolution:
$$\text{Steps/rev} = \frac{360°}{15°} = 24 \text{ steps/rev}$$

Pulses per second = 20 → Steps per second = 20

Shaft speed in rev/sec = 20 / 24 = 0.833 rev/sec

Shaft speed in RPM = 0.833 × 60 = **50 RPM**

**Step 3: Pulses Required for 90° Rotation**

Number of steps for 90° = $\dfrac{90°}{15°/\text{step}} = 6 \text{ steps}$

Since one step requires one pulse, **6 pulses** are needed to rotate the shaft by 90°.

**Final Answers:** Step angle = **15°**, Shaft speed = **50 RPM**, Pulses for 90° = **6 pulses**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams (Module 1):**
>
> 1. **Confusing the number of parallel paths (A):** For **lap winding**, $A = P$ (number of poles). For **wave winding**, $A = 2$. Mixing these up will give a wrong speed value.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversions:** Pressure in **bar** must be converted to **Pa** (multiply by $10^5$). Flow rate in **LPM** must be converted to **m³/s** (divide by 60000). Students lose 1–2 marks by writing final answers in the wrong unit.
> 3. **Order of RPY multiplication:** The matrix multiplication $R_z(\psi) R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi)$ is **NOT commutative**. Reversing the order gives a completely different orientation. Always state the convention used.
> 4. **Step angle formula for VR stepper:** Many students use $\beta = 360°/N_r$ instead of $\beta = 360°(N_s - N_r)/(N_s N_r)$. The first formula is wrong unless the stator and rotor tooth counts differ by 1.
> 5. **Power Transmission Direction:** Velocity ratio = $N_{in}/N_{out} = T_{out}/T_{in}$ (for an ideal system). Writing it inverted is a common error.
> 6. **Efficiency inclusion in output torque:** Students often forget to multiply the ideal torque amplification by the efficiency. Always state the formula as $T_{out} = T_{in} \times i \times \eta$.
> 7. **Confusing DOF and joint count:** A 6-DOF robot has 6 **actuated joints**, not 6 motors (one motor per joint, but some designs use differential drives).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Fire Revision Checklist

- [x] **Roll (φ) is rotation about the X-axis**, **Pitch (θ) is rotation about the Y-axis**, and **Yaw (ψ) is rotation about the Z-axis** (right-hand rule).
- [x] A 6-DOF robot needs **3 translational + 3 rotational** DOFs; the rotational set is Roll, Pitch, Yaw.
- [x] The RPY composite rotation matrix is $R = R_z(\psi) R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi)$ – this order matters.
- [x] Robot drives are classified into **Electric, Hydraulic, and Pneumatic** – know one application for each.
- [x] **DC Motor voltage equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$. **Back EMF:** $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$. **Torque:** $T = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$.
- [x] **No-load speed** $N_0 = \dfrac{60 A V}{P \Phi Z}$; **Stall torque** $T_{stall} = \dfrac{P \Phi Z V}{2 \pi A R_a}$.
- [x] DC motor speed-torque characteristic is a **straight line** with negative slope: $N = N_0 (1 - T/T_{stall})$.
- [x] **Stepper motor step angle:** $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{\text{steps per rev}}$. Standard resolutions: **0.9°, 1.8°, 3.75°, 7.5°, 15°**.
- [x] VR stepper step angle: $\beta = \dfrac{360° (N_s - N_r)}{N_s N_r}$.
- [x] Stepper motors provide **open-loop position control** – no encoder needed; pulse count = position.
- [x] **Microstepping** divides each full step into 2, 4, 8, …, 256 sub-steps for finer resolution.
- [x] **Hydraulic motor torque:** $T = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$. **Speed:** $N = \dfrac{Q}{V_D}$. **Power:** $P = \Delta P \cdot Q = T \cdot \omega$.
- [x] **Hydraulic motor types:** Gear, Vane, Piston (axial/radial). Piston motors are most efficient.
- [x] **Power transmission** modifies the speed-torque curve: high ratio → low speed, high torque.
- [x] **Gear velocity ratio:** $VR = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_1} = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ (ideal).
- [x] **Belt drive velocity:** $V = \pi D_1 N_1 = \pi D_2 N_2$. **Chain drive** is similar to belt drive but uses sprockets.
- [x] **Harmonic drive** provides very high reduction ratios (30:1 to 320:1) in a single stage, used in robot wrists.
- [x] **Total transmission ratio** is the **product** of individual stage ratios. **Total efficiency** is the **product** of individual stage efficiencies.
- [x] **Hydraulic robots** (e.g., Unimate 2000) carry heavy payloads (100–500 kg); **Electric robots** (e.g., SCARA) are precise for light assembly; **Pneumatic robots** are for simple pick-and-place.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always draw a **neat, labelled diagram** first (1–2 marks reserved for diagrams). Then derive formulas step-by-step, **substitute values with units**, and **box the final answer with the correct unit**. The examiner will look for a clean structure: **Given → Formula → Substitution → Result**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position Sensor, Potentiometer, Encoders, LVDT, Velocity Sensor, Acceleration Sensor, Force, Pressure and Torque Sensor, Touch and Tactile Sensor, Proximity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT721 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS & AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Position Sensor, Potentiometer, Encoders, LVDT, Velocity Sensor, Acceleration Sensor, Force, Pressure and Torque Sensor, Touch and Tactile Sensor, Proximity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Family of Robotic Sensors

A **sensor** is a transduction device that converts a physical quantity (displacement, force, temperature, light) into a measurable electrical signal. In robotics, sensors form the **Perception Layer** of the robot control architecture, enabling feedback control, environment awareness, and safe human-robot interaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Sensors are classified by the physical variable they measure. For Module 2, the focus is on **internal-state sensors** (proprioceptors) and **external-state sensors** (exteroceptors) that interface with the robot controller (PLC / microcontroller / DSP).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Robot

Imagine a human body. Your **eyes, skin, ears, and inner-ear balance organs** are sensors. Similarly, a robot needs:
- **Position sensors** → like your joint-awareness (proprioception)
- **Velocity sensors** → like your cerebellum (motion tracking)
- **Acceleration sensors** → like your inner-ear vestibular system
- **Force/Torque sensors** → like your hand grip awareness
- **Touch/Tactile sensors** → like your skin
- **Proximity sensors** → like obstacle awareness before collision

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Categories Covered in Module 2:**
> 1. **Position Sensors** — Potentiometer, Encoders (Incremental & Absolute), LVDT
> 2. **Velocity Sensors** — Tachogenerator, Encoder-based
> 3. **Acceleration Sensors** — Piezoelectric & MEMS Accelerometers
> 4. **Force / Pressure / Torque Sensors** — Strain gauge, Piezoelectric, Load cells
> 5. **Touch & Tactile Sensors** — Resistive, Capacitive, Piezoelectric arrays
> 6. **Proximity Sensors** — Inductive, Capacitive, Optical, Ultrasonic, Magnetic (Hall-effect)

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Displacement | $x$ | **m (meter)** |
| Velocity | $v$ | **m/s** |
| Acceleration | $a$ | **m/s²** |
| Force | $F$ | **N (Newton)** |
| Pressure | $P$ | **Pa (Pascal)** |
| Torque | $\tau$ | **N·m** |
| Strain | $\varepsilon$ | **dimensionless** |
| Sensitivity | $S$ | **V/unit (input)** |
| Resolution | $r$ | **smallest detectable step** |
| Range | — | **min–max measurable value** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Position Sensor — Potentiometer

A **potentiometer** is a passive resistive position sensor with three terminals. A wiper slides along a resistive element, tapping off a voltage divider output proportional to displacement.

**Operating Principle (Voltage Divider):**

Let the total resistance be $R_{tot}$, and the wiper be at position $x$ along a track of total length $L$. The resistance between one end and the wiper is $R_x$.

$$
R_x = R_{tot} \cdot \frac{x}{L}
$$

The output voltage (with $V_{ref}$ applied across the element) is:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_{tot}} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{x}{L}
$$

**Sensitivity:**

$$
S_{pot} = \frac{dV_{out}}{dx} = \frac{V_{ref}}{L} \quad [\text{V/m}]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Point:** A linear potentiometer gives a **linear V–x characteristic** (ideal). In practice, the **load error** caused by a finite output-load resistance $R_L$ must be considered.

**Load Error:**

$$
\varepsilon_{load} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{R_{tot}}{R_L} \cdot \frac{x}{L}\left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right)}
$$

### 2.2 Encoders

An **encoder** is a digital position sensor that produces discrete pulses (or unique codes) corresponding to angular or linear displacement.

#### 2.2.1 Incremental Encoder

A rotating disc with **N slots** produces N pulses per revolution. Position is determined by **counting pulses** from a reference (index) mark.

$$
\text{Resolution (angular)} = \theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{N}
$$

$$
\text{Linear resolution} = \frac{\pi D}{N} \quad [\text{m/pulse}]
$$

where $D$ is the wheel/drum diameter.

**Quadrature decoding** uses two channels (A, B) phase-shifted by **90°** to determine direction and quadruple the resolution:

$$
\theta_{quad} = \frac{360°}{4N}
$$

#### 2.2.2 Absolute Encoder

A multi-track disc with **n bits** gives $2^n$ unique positions.

$$
N_{positions} = 2^n
$$

$$
\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{2^n}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For an 8-bit absolute encoder, $N_{positions} = 256$ and $\theta_{res} = 1.40625°$ per step. *Memorize the $2^n$ relationship.*

### 2.3 LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)

The **LVDT** is a passive, contactless, inductive position sensor with **infinite resolution** (limited only by signal conditioning). It has a primary coil and two secondary coils wound on a hollow cylindrical former; a movable ferromagnetic core slides inside.

**Output Voltage (Displacement $x$):**

$$
V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot (V_{S2} - V_{S1})
$$

For a perfectly symmetric LVDT:

$$
V_{out}(x) = K_{LVDT} \cdot x \quad \text{(linear range)}
$$

Outside the linear range, the response is non-linear. The output is also **phase-sensitive** — the sign of $x$ is determined by whether $V_{out}$ leads or lags the primary excitation by 90°.

| Feature | LVDT Property |
|---|---|
| Range | ±0.1 mm to ±500 mm |
| Resolution | < 0.1 µm (with good electronics) |
| Excitation | 1–10 V at 1–10 kHz |
| Output | AC, needs demodulation |
| Linearity | ±0.1 % to ±0.25 % of full scale |

### 2.4 Velocity Sensor — Tachogenerator

A **tachogenerator** is a small DC/AC generator whose output voltage is proportional to angular velocity.

$$
V_{tacho} = K_v \cdot \omega \quad [\text{V} = (\text{V·s/rad}) \cdot \text{rad/s}]
$$

For an **AC (induction) tachogenerator**, the output is sinusoidal with **frequency = pole-pairs × ω / (2π)** and amplitude proportional to ω.

### 2.5 Acceleration Sensor — Accelerometer

A **piezoelectric / MEMS accelerometer** consists of a *proof mass* $m$ suspended by a spring (stiffness $k$) with damping $b$. Under acceleration $a$, the proof mass displaces by $z$ relative to the housing.

**Equation of Motion (Force Balance on Mass):**

$$
m \ddot{z} + b \dot{z} + k z = -m \cdot a(t)
$$

The displacement of the proof mass is:

$$
z = \frac{-m \cdot a}{k} \quad \text{(steady-state, } \omega \to 0 \text{)}
$$

Sensitivity:

$$
S_{acc} = \frac{dz}{da} = \frac{-m}{k} \quad [\text{m/(m/s²)}]
$$

The **natural frequency** and **damping ratio** are:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{b}{2\sqrt{km}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** For measuring DC or low-frequency acceleration, the displacement-type accelerometer's output is **linear with acceleration only for $\omega \ll \omega_n$**. A piezoelectric accelerometer measures the *stress* (proportional to $a$) and is suited for **dynamic** acceleration (high-frequency vibration), not DC.

### 2.6 Force / Pressure / Torque Sensor

#### 2.6.1 Strain Gauge (Force Sensor Core)

A **strain gauge** is a thin metallic foil whose resistance changes with mechanical strain $\varepsilon$.

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon
$$

where $G_F$ is the **gauge factor** (typically **2.0 to 2.2** for constantan, ~ **100+** for semiconductor).

For a bar under axial load $F$ with cross-section $A$ and Young's modulus $E$:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{F}{A \cdot E}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \frac{F}{A \cdot E}
$$

The change in resistance is converted to voltage using a **Wheatstone bridge** with excitation $V_{ex}$:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \left(\frac{\Delta R_1}{R_1} - \frac{\Delta R_2}{R_2} + \frac{\Delta R_3}{R_3} - \frac{\Delta R_4}{R_4}\right)
$$

#### 2.6.2 Pressure Sensor

Pressure $P$ applied to a diaphragm of area $A$ produces force $F = P \cdot A$ that strains the diaphragm. A strain gauge bonded to the diaphragm transduces strain to electrical output:

$$
V_{out} \propto P
$$

**Piezoelectric pressure sensor** directly produces a charge $Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot P \cdot A$, where $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (C/N).

#### 2.6.3 Torque Sensor

A **reaction-type torque sensor** uses a shaft section with bonded strain gauges. For a shaft of polar moment $J$, length $L$, shear modulus $G$ under torque $T$:

$$
\varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3 G}
$$

The 45° rosette strain gauges convert this shear strain into a measurable voltage.

### 2.7 Touch and Tactile Sensor

A **tactile sensor** is an array of sensing elements providing distributed pressure information.

| Type | Working Principle |
|---|---|
| **Resistive** | Contact pressure $\downarrow$ resistance $\Rightarrow$ current $\uparrow$ |
| **Capacitive** | Pressure changes gap $d$: $C = \varepsilon A / d$ |
| **Piezoelectric** | Pressure $\Rightarrow$ charge $Q = d \cdot F$ |
| **Optical** | Pressure deforms elastomer $\Rightarrow$ light intensity changes |
| **Quantum Tunneling** | Contact causes electron tunneling (very sensitive) |

For a capacitive tactile element:

$$
C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}
$$

Pressure that reduces $d$ increases $C$, which is read as a voltage change via an $RC$ circuit.

### 2.8 Proximity Sensor

A **proximity sensor** detects the presence of an object *without physical contact*, typically within **0–10 mm** range (short-range).

| Type | Detects | Range | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inductive** | Metallic only | 0.5–60 mm | PNP/NPN switching |
| **Capacitive** | Any material (dielectric) | 1–50 mm | Switching or analog |
| **Optical (diffuse)** | Any opaque | 5–200 mm | Switching |
| **Ultrasonic** | Any material | 50–3000 mm | Switching / analog |
| **Magnetic (Hall-effect)** | Magnets / ferromagnetic | 1–30 mm | Switching |

**Hall-Effect Sensor Output Voltage:**

$$
V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}
$$

where $R_H$ is the Hall coefficient, $I$ is the bias current, $B$ is the magnetic flux density, and $t$ is the sensor thickness.

### 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

| Sensor | Core Formula | Key Variable | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Potentiometer | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot x/L$ | $x$ = displacement | m |
| Incremental Encoder | $\theta_{res} = 360°/N$ | $N$ = pulses/rev | dimensionless |
| Absolute Encoder | $N_{pos} = 2^n$ | $n$ = bit count | dimensionless |
| LVDT | $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$ | $x$ in linear range | m |
| Tachogenerator | $V_{tacho} = K_v \cdot \omega$ | $\omega$ | rad/s |
| Accelerometer | $z = -ma/k$ (static) | $a$ | m/s² |
| Strain Gauge | $\Delta R / R = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G_F \approx 2$ | dimensionless |
| Wheatstone Bridge | $V_{out} = (V_{ex}/4) \Sigma (\pm \Delta R_i / R_i)$ | $V_{ex}$ | V |
| Pressure (piezo) | $Q = d \cdot P \cdot A$ | $d$ = piezo coeff | C/N |
| Capacitive tactile | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | $d$ = gap | m |
| Hall sensor | $V_H = R_H I B / t$ | $B$ = flux density | T |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Industrial robots (e.g., FANUC, KUKA) use **absolute encoders** on each joint for home-position recovery after power-off. Collaborative robots (cobots) use **torque sensors** in every joint for safe human-contact detection. Surgical robots use **LVDT** for sub-mm precision.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Potentiometer Transfer Function Under Load

**Problem:** A linear potentiometer with $R_{tot} = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ is connected to a load $R_L = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$. The wiper is at $x = 0.4L$. $V_{ref} = 10\ \text{V}$. Find the loaded output voltage and the load error.

**Step 1 — Identify the two halves of the potentiometer.**

$$
R_1 = R_{tot} \cdot \frac{x}{L} = 10 \cdot 0.4 = 4\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
R_2 = R_{tot} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right) = 10 \cdot 0.6 = 6\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Model the load as a parallel combination with $R_2$ (lower half).**

$$
R_{2,eq} = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_L}{R_2 + R_L} = \frac{6 \cdot 100}{6 + 100} = 5.6604\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the voltage divider with the loaded half.**

$$
V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{2,eq}}{R_1 + R_{2,eq}} = 10 \cdot \frac{5.6604}{4 + 5.6604} = 10 \cdot \frac{5.6604}{9.6604}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 5.860\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the ideal (unloaded) output.**

$$
V_{out,ideal} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{x}{L} = 10 \cdot 0.4 = 4.0\ \text{V} \quad \text{(using the upper half)}
$$

$$
\text{Wait — re-derive the correct unloaded value:}
$$

The unloaded ideal depends on the chosen reference. If $V_{ref}$ is applied across the full element and output is taken from the wiper to one end:

$$
V_{out,ideal} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{x}{L} = 10 \cdot 0.4 = 4.0\ \text{V}
$$

> The **5.86 V** result above is wrong because the load is across $R_2$ (between wiper and ground), so the correct divider is across $R_1$ (wiper to the other end). Let us **re-compute** correctly.

**Corrected Step 2:** Load is between wiper and ground (across $R_2$):

$$
R_{2,eq} = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_L}{R_2 + R_L} = 5.6604\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Corrected Step 3:** The wiper voltage = voltage across $R_{2,eq}$:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{2,eq}}{R_1 + R_{2,eq}} = 10 \cdot \frac{5.6604}{4 + 5.6604} = 5.860\ \text{V}
$$

> This is greater than the **ideal 4.0 V**, which is mathematically impossible for a pure divider. The error signals a sign convention. The wiper voltage with the load should be **less than** the ideal when the load is finite. The correct voltage is across $R_1$ if $V_{ref}$ is across the lower half — or we accept the non-linear curve. The **lesson**: load error analysis must use the correct wiper reference node.

**Step 5 — Formally express the load error using the textbook formula.**

$$
V_{out,loaded} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{\frac{x}{L}}{1 + \frac{R_{tot}}{R_L}\left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right) \frac{x}{L}}
$$

Substituting $R_{tot}/R_L = 10/100 = 0.1$, $x/L = 0.4$:

$$
V_{out} = 10 \cdot \frac{0.4}{1 + 0.1 \cdot (0.6)(0.4)} = 10 \cdot \frac{0.4}{1.024} = 3.906\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute load error.**

$$
\varepsilon_{load} = \frac{V_{out,loaded} - V_{out,ideal}}{V_{out,ideal}} = \frac{3.906 - 4.000}{4.000} = -0.0234 = -2.34\%
$$

**Result:** The potentiometer with finite load reads **3.906 V** instead of 4.0 V — a **2.34% low error**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: LVDT Sensitivity and Linear Range

**Given:** An LVDT has primary excitation $V_p = 5\ \text{V rms}$ at $f = 5\ \text{kHz}$. The secondary coils produce $V_{S1}$ and $V_{S2}$ such that $|V_{S1} - V_{S2}|$ is **2.0 V per mm** of core displacement (in linear range). Find the output voltage when the core is displaced **+3.5 mm** and **−2.0 mm**.

**Step 1 — Define sensitivity.**

$$
K_{LVDT} = 2.0\ \text{V/mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply for $x = +3.5$ mm.**

$$
V_{out}(+3.5) = K_{LVDT} \cdot x = 2.0 \cdot 3.5 = +7.0\ \text{V rms}
$$

The output is **in phase** with the primary excitation.

**Step 3 — Apply for $x = -2.0$ mm.**

$$
V_{out}(-2.0) = 2.0 \cdot (-2.0) = -4.0\ \text{V rms}
$$

The output is **180° out of phase** (because $V_{S2} > V_{S1}$). A phase-sensitive demodulator recovers the sign.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** The LVDT is inherently a **bipolar** sensor — the *sign* of $V_{out}$ encodes the *direction* of displacement. This is its main advantage over a simple potentiometer.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Strain-Gauge Load Cell Output

**Given:** A load cell uses four strain gauges (two in tension, two in compression) in a full Wheatstone bridge. Excitation $V_{ex} = 10\ \text{V}$, gauge factor $G_F = 2.0$, modulus $E = 200\ \text{GPa}$, cross-section $A = 100\ \text{mm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$. Applied force $F = 5000\ \text{N}$. Find $V_{out}$.

**Step 1 — Compute strain.**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{F}{A \cdot E} = \frac{5000}{100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 200 \times 10^9} = \frac{5000}{20 \times 10^6} = 250 \times 10^{-6} = 250\ \mu\varepsilon
$$

**Step 2 — Resistance change in each active gauge.**

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon = 2.0 \cdot 250 \times 10^{-6} = 500 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the full-bridge formula (two tensile, two compressive, hence double the effect).**

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = 10 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-4} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} = 5\ \text{mV}
$$

**Step 4 — Sensitivity of the load cell.**

$$
S_{cell} = \frac{V_{out}}{F} = \frac{5\ \text{mV}}{5000\ \text{N}} = 1\ \mu\text{V/N}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Sensor (KTU Application Question)

| Application | Recommended Sensor | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Robot joint position (home return) | **Absolute optical encoder** | Retains position after power-off |
| Robot joint velocity control | **Incremental encoder + frequency-to-voltage** | High resolution, digital output |
| Pneumatic cylinder rod position | **Linear potentiometer** | Cheap, sufficient resolution |
| Surgical robot tool tip (sub-mm) | **LVDT** | High resolution, frictionless |
| Mobile robot obstacle detection (long range) | **Ultrasonic / LiDAR** | Detects non-metals, long range |
| Conveyor metal part counting | **Inductive proximity** | Robust, cheap, contactless |
| Robot gripper force feedback | **Strain-gauge based force sensor** | Direct force measurement |
| Vibration monitoring of a motor | **Piezoelectric accelerometer** | Wide frequency response |
| Plastic bottle detection on conveyor | **Capacitive proximity** | Detects non-metals |
| Welding robot seam tracking | **Optical / inductive (for arc)** | High temperature resistant |

```mermaid

    Start[What do you need to measure?]
```
