## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT). Mention two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

The LVDT consists of a **primary coil** and **two secondary coils** (S1 and S2) wound symmetrically on a hollow cylindrical former. A movable **ferromagnetic core** slides inside. The primary is excited by an AC voltage $V_p$ (1–10 kHz, 1–10 V).

**Working:**
- When the core is at the **null (center) position**, $|V_{S1}| = |V_{S2}|$, so the differential output $V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2} = 0$.
- When the core moves **towards S1**, the flux linkage with S1 increases, so $V_{S1} > V_{S2}$, and $V_{out}$ is **in phase** with $V_p$.
- When the core moves **towards S2**, $V_{S2} > V_{S1}$, and $V_{out}$ is **180° out of phase** with $V_p$.
- Within the linear range, $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$.

**Advantages (any 2):**
1. **Infinite resolution** (limited only by signal conditioning electronics).
2. **Frictionless and contactless** — very long life.
3. **Bipolar output** — sign indicates direction.
4. **High linearity** (±0.1 % of full scale).

**(3 marks: 1 diagram + 1 working + 2 advantages)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between incremental and absolute encoders.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Pulses (A, B, optional Z index) | Unique n-bit code per position |
| Position on power-up | Lost — must home | Retained |
| Resolution | $360°/N$ (per rev) | $360°/2^n$ |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Tracks on disc | 1 or 2 (quadrature) | $n$ tracks |
| Use case | Velocity, position w/ homing | Home-return robots, CNC |
| Direction sense | Via A-B phase | Implicit in code |

Incremental encoders need a **reference (index) pulse** and a **homing routine** to define zero. Absolute encoders give true position even after power-off — crucial in **robotics and CNC**.

**(3 marks: tabular comparison with 4-5 key points)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Provided (Module 2)**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a **piezoelectric accelerometer**. Derive the equation of motion of the proof mass and explain how the natural frequency affects the usable range. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A strain-gauge based load cell uses four active gauges in a full bridge. The cell is rated for 50 kN. If $G_F = 2.1$, $E = 200\ \text{GPa}$, and the bar cross-section is $A = 250\ \text{mm}^2$, find the bridge output at full load with $V_{ex} = 10\ \text{V}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Piezoelectric Accelerometer — Working and Derivation:**

**Construction:** A piezoelectric accelerometer consists of a **proof mass** $m$ clamped against a **piezoelectric crystal** (typically PZT or quartz), all housed in a metal case. When the housing accelerates, the inertial mass exerts a force $F = ma$ on the piezo crystal, which produces a charge $Q = d \cdot F$, where $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (C/N).

**Derivation — Equation of Motion:**

Let $x$ be the housing displacement, $z$ the *relative* displacement of the mass with respect to the housing, and $y = z + x$ the absolute displacement of the mass. The forces on the mass are: spring force $-k z$, damping force $-b \dot{z}$, and the reaction from the base. Newton's second law gives:

$$
m \ddot{y} = -k z - b \dot{z}
$$

Since $y = z + x$, $\ddot{y} = \ddot{z} + \ddot{x} = \ddot{z} + a(t)$, where $a(t)$ is the housing acceleration. Substituting:

$$
m \ddot{z} + b \dot{z} + k z = -m \cdot a(t)
$$

**Transfer Function** (in Laplace domain):

$$
\frac{Z(s)}{A(s)} = \frac{-m}{m s^2 + b s + k} = \frac{-1}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2}
$$

where $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and $\zeta = b/(2\sqrt{km})$.

**Quasi-static behavior** ($\omega \ll \omega_n$): $Z/A \approx -m/k$, i.e., the relative displacement is proportional to acceleration. The output of the piezoelectric element (charge) is:

$$
Q = d \cdot k \cdot z = -d \cdot m \cdot a(t)
$$

**Usable Range:** The accelerometer must operate **well below $\omega_n$** (typically $\omega < 0.3\,\omega_n$) to avoid resonance amplification. Therefore, **piezoelectric accelerometers are unsuitable for DC (zero-frequency) measurements** — they measure only **dynamic** acceleration.

> **Marks split:** 2 marks block diagram, 3 marks derivation, 2 marks natural-frequency discussion.

---

**(b) Load Cell Bridge Output — Numerical:**

**Given:**
- Full load $F = 50\ \text{kN} = 50{,}000\ \text{N}$
- $G_F = 2.1$
- $E = 200\ \text{GPa} = 200 \times 10^9\ \text{Pa}$
- $A = 250\ \text{mm}^2 = 250 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$
- $V_{ex} = 10\ \text{V}$
- Full bridge (4 active gauges)

**Step 1 — Compute strain at full load:**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{F}{A E} = \frac{50{,}000}{250 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 200 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{50{,}000}{50 \times 10^6} = 1 \times 10^{-3} = 1000\ \mu\varepsilon
$$

**Step 2 — Compute relative resistance change in each gauge:**

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply full-bridge formula.**

For a full bridge with **two gauges in tension and two in compression**, the differential output is:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = 10 \cdot 2.1 \times 10^{-3} = 21\ \text{mV}
$$

**Step 4 — Sensitivity:**

$$
S = \frac{21\ \text{mV}}{50\ \text{kN}} = 0.42\ \mu\text{V/N}
$$

> **Marks split:** 2 marks formula, 2 marks strain calculation, 2 marks resistance change, 1 mark output.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the working of an **incremental optical encoder** with a neat sketch. Define **quadrature decoding** and explain how it quadruples the resolution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot wheel of diameter **120 mm** is fitted with an incremental encoder of **500 pulses/rev**. The encoder count reads an increase of **2400 pulses** in **0.1 s**. Calculate the **linear distance travelled**, the **linear speed** of the robot, and the **angular velocity** of the wheel. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Incremental Optical Encoder:**

**Construction:** A disc with $N$ radial slots is mounted on the rotating shaft. A **light source (LED)** is on one side, and a **photodetector** on the other. As the disc rotates, slots alternately pass and block the light, producing a **pulse train** at the photodetector output.

**Output Channels:**
- **Channel A**: pulse train
- **Channel B**: pulse train phase-shifted by 90° (electrical quadrature)
- **Channel Z (index)**: one pulse per revolution for reference/homing

**Quadrature Decoding:** The 90° phase shift between A and B allows the direction of rotation to be detected:
- If **A leads B**: clockwise rotation
- If **B leads A**: counter-clockwise rotation

By counting **all four edges** (rising A, falling A, rising B, falling B), the resolution is **quadrupled**:

$$
\text{Resolution with quadrature} = \frac{360°}{4N} \quad \text{(or 4 counts per slot)}
$$

**Resolution Example:** For $N = 500$ pulses/rev:
- Without quadrature: $360°/500 = 0.72°$/pulse
- With quadrature: $360°/2000 = 0.18°$/pulse

> **Marks split:** 2 marks sketch, 2 marks operation, 3 marks quadrature explanation.

---

**(b) Robot Wheel Kinematics — Numerical:**

**Given:**
- Wheel diameter $D = 120\ \text{mm} = 0.12\ \text{m}$
- Encoder pulses/rev $N = 500$
- Pulse count $\Delta n = 2400$ in $\Delta t = 0.1\ \text{s}$

**Step 1 — Number of revolutions:**

$$
N_{rev} = \frac{\Delta n}{N} = \frac{2400}{500} = 4.8\ \text{revolutions}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear distance travelled:**

The wheel circumference is:

$$
C = \pi D = \pi \cdot 0.12 = 0.3770\ \text{m/rev}
$$

$$
s = N_{rev} \cdot C = 4.8 \cdot 0.3770 = 1.8096\ \text{m} \approx 1.81\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Linear speed:**

$$
v = \frac{s}{\Delta t} = \frac{1.8096}{0.1} = 18.096\ \text{m/s} \approx 18.1\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Angular velocity of the wheel:**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot N_{rev}}{\Delta t} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 4.8}{0.1} = 301.6\ \text{rad/s}
$$

Alternatively:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot N}{\Delta t \cdot \text{count/pulse}} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 2400}{500 \cdot 0.1} = 301.6\ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Express in RPM:**

$$
\text{RPM} = \frac{\omega \cdot 60}{2\pi} = \frac{301.6 \cdot 60}{2\pi} = 2880\ \text{RPM}
$$

> **Marks split:** 1 mark revolutions, 2 marks distance, 2 marks speed, 2 marks angular velocity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in LVDT:** Forgetting to mention the *phase reversal* that gives direction. *(−1 to −2 marks)*
> 2. **Quadrature factor confusion:** Some students divide by 2 instead of multiplying by 4 in quadrature resolution. Re-read: **4 edges per cycle**.
> 3. **Strain gauge with wrong gauge factor:** Using $G_F = 1$ (default) when the problem gives $G_F = 2.1$. *Always re-check the problem statement.*
> 4. **Wheatstone bridge formula sign:** Forgetting that a full bridge **doubles** the half-bridge output. The output is $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot (\Delta R / R)$, **not** $V_{ex} \cdot (\Delta R / 4R)$.
> 5. **Accelerometer range:** Confusing MEMS (DC-capable) with piezoelectric (AC-only). This is a 1-mark distinction in 3-mark questions.
> 6. **Unit mismatch in tachogenerator:** $\omega$ must be in **rad/s**, not RPM, when used in $V = K_v \cdot \omega$.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Potentiometer** → analog V–x relation, $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot (x/L)$; suffers **load error**.
- [ ] **Incremental encoder** → $N$ pulses/rev, resolution $360°/N$; **quadrature = 4×**.
- [ ] **Absolute encoder** → $2^n$ unique positions, retains data on power-off.
- [ ] **LVDT** → AC excitation, phase-sensitive demodulation, infinite resolution, bipolar.
- [ ] **Tachogenerator** → $V = K_v \cdot \omega$; DC or AC type.
- [ ] **Accelerometer** → proof-mass on spring: $m\ddot{z} + b\dot{z} + kz = -ma(t)$; operates **below $\omega_n$**.
- [ ] **Piezoelectric accelerometer** → measures **dynamic** acceleration only (no DC).
- [ ] **Strain gauge** → $\Delta R / R = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$; **Wheatstone bridge** required.
- [ ] **Load cell** → full-bridge gives 4× the sensitivity of a single gauge.
- [ ] **Pressure sensor** → diaphragm + strain gauge, $V_{out} \propto P$.
- [ ] **Torque sensor** → 45° rosette on shaft; measures shear strain $\propto T$.
- [ ] **Tactile sensor** → array of capacitive / resistive / piezoelectric cells; gives distributed pressure.
- [ ] **Inductive proximity** → detects **metals only**; uses eddy-current losses.
- [ ] **Capacitive proximity** → detects **any material** (dielectric change).
- [ ] **Hall-effect sensor** → detects **magnetic field**; $V_H = R_H I B / t$.
- [ ] **Ultrasonic sensor** → long-range, all materials, time-of-flight principle.
- [ ] **Optical proximity (diffuse)** → short-range, all opaque materials.
- [ ] **KTU Hot Words:** "transducer", "transduction principle", "range", "resolution", "sensitivity", "linearity", "hysteresis", "bandwidth", "contact vs contactless", "active vs passive".
- [ ] **Always state the units** in the final answer (V, m, rad/s, m/s², N) — losing units = losing 0.5–1 mark.
- [ ] **Draw the block diagram** wherever a working principle is asked — marks are reserved for diagrams even when not explicitly mentioned.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Range Sensors

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)
A **Range Sensor** is a non-contact or contact-based transducer device mounted on a robotic manipulator that measures the **distance (range)** between the sensor head and a target object, surface, or obstacle in the robot's workspace. Range sensors form the perceptual backbone of proximity detection, obstacle avoidance, and SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) operations in modern robotic systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** A range sensor is classified as any device that provides an output signal (analog voltage, digital pulse, or data stream) that is a monotonic function of the distance `d` between the sensor and the measured object.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **range sensor as the "eyes" of a robot** in a dark, foggy warehouse. Just as a person walking down a corridor in pitch darkness would extend their hand, tap a wall, or shout to hear an echo (and thereby know how far the wall is), a robot uses a range sensor to actively or passively probe the environment and determine "**how far is the next obstacle?**" 

For example, a **batsman on a cricket field** uses echoes to know the distance to the stumps — this is exactly how **ultrasonic range sensors** work, sending out sound pulses and timing their return.

### Key Standard Metrics
- **Measurement Range:** Typically **50 mm to 6000 mm** (industrial short-range), up to **200 m** (LiDAR).
- **Accuracy:** ±1 mm to ±10 mm depending on technology.
- **Response Time:** **10 ms to 100 ms**.
- **Beam Angle:** **1° to 30°** (LiDAR narrow, ultrasonic wide).

---

## 1.2 Sniff Sensors

### Formal Definition
A **Sniff Sensor** (also termed **Electronic Nose / E-Nose** or **Chemical Sensor Array**) is a robotic sensory device that detects, identifies, and quantifies **chemical compounds, gases, vapors, or volatile organic compounds (VOCs)** in the surrounding atmosphere using an array of partially selective chemical sensors coupled with a pattern recognition system (often AI/Neural Network-based).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Sniff sensors are classified under the broader category of **non-optical, non-tactile, non-range** environmental sensors — they add the dimension of "**chemical perception**" to robots, enabling applications in leak detection, food quality, explosives detection, and biomedical breath analysis.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **highly trained sniffer dog at an airport** — it can detect a single molecule of an explosive hidden inside a suitcase. A **sniff sensor is the robotic version of this dog's nose**. It doesn't just detect "smell" — it identifies the **chemical signature** of the smell using multiple gas sensors (like MQ-3, MQ-7, TGS series), each sensitive to a different gas family. The combined response pattern acts like a unique "fingerprint" of the chemical.

---

## 1.3 Robot End Effectors

### Formal Definition
A **Robot End Effector (REE)** is the **special-purpose tooling or device** mounted at the **wrist or tool plate** of a robotic manipulator's terminal link (the end of the kinematic chain). It is the **only component of the robot that physically interacts with the external environment** and is responsible for executing the designated task — grasping, welding, painting, drilling, or sensing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** "The end effector is the **last link** of the robotic manipulator, designed and interfaced as per the **task specification**. It transforms the generalized motion of the robot arm into a **specific, useful work action**."

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of the end effector as the **"hand" of the robot** — but unlike a human hand, a robot's "hand" is task-specific. A surgeon uses a **scalpel** for one task and **forceps** for another; similarly, a robot swaps its end effector (via a quick-change coupling) depending on whether it must **pick, weld, paint, or drill**.

---

## 1.4 Types of End Effectors

End effectors are broadly classified into two super-categories based on function:

| Category | Function | Examples |
|----------|----------|----------|
| **Grippers (Mechanical/Soft)** | Grasp, hold, manipulate objects | Two-finger, three-finger, parallel jaw, angular jaw |
| **Tools (Non-Grasping)** | Perform a process action | Spot welding gun, paint sprayer, drilling spindle, polishing wheel, heat gun |
| **Sensor End Effectors** | Sense the environment at the wrist | Force/torque sensor, vision camera, range sensor, sniff sensor |
| **Specialized/Combination** | Hybrid functions | Gripper with built-in vision, multi-tool turret |

---

## 1.5 Mechanical Gripper

### Formal Definition
A **Mechanical Gripper** is a type of end effector that uses **mechanically actuated jaws or fingers** to grasp, hold, and release a workpiece by applying a controlled **gripping force** through physical contact with the object's surfaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Mechanical grippers are the **most commonly deployed end effector in industry**, accounting for approximately **40–50%** of all robotic manipulation tasks, particularly in pick-and-place, assembly, and packaging operations.

### Conceptual Analogy
A **mechanical gripper is like a pair of pliers or a nutcracker** — it has two fingers (jaws) that close onto an object. The harder the squeezing action (actuator force), the firmer the grip. Spring-loaded, pneumatic, hydraulic, or servo-electric actuators drive this motion.

---

## 1.6 Types of Gripper Mechanism

Gripper mechanisms are classified based on the **source of gripping force** and the **physical principle** used:

1. **Mechanical (Jaw-type) Grippers** — Pneumatic, hydraulic, or electric actuation
2. **Magnetic Grippers** — Electromagnet or permanent magnet
3. **Vacuum (Suction) Grippers** — Negative pressure cups
4. **Adhesive Grippers** — Compliant adhesive pads (gecko-inspired)
5. **Hook/Catenary Grippers** — Simple hook engagement
6. **Soft/Fluidic Grippers** — Inflatable elastomer chambers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "list and explain types of grippers," always classify them under the heading **"By Gripping Force Source"** — this is the examiner-preferred taxonomy in the 2024 scheme.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Range Sensors — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

Range sensors operate on **five principal physical principles**. Each principle yields a different engineering trade-off in terms of **range, accuracy, cost, and target material compatibility**.

### A. Ultrasonic Range Sensors
- **Principle:** Time-of-Flight (ToF) of acoustic waves at ~**40 kHz** (above human hearing).
- **Logic Steps:**
  1. The transmitter emits an ultrasonic pulse.
  2. The pulse travels through air at the speed of sound `c ≈ 343 m/s`.
  3. The pulse strikes an object and reflects (echo).
  4. The receiver detects the echo and the timer measures the round-trip time `t`.
  5. The distance is computed as `d = (c × t) / 2`.
- **Pros:** Cheap, works on any material (transparent, metallic, plastic, liquid), immune to dust/fog.
- **Cons:** Wide beam angle (~15°–30°), poor accuracy on soft/angled surfaces, slow update rate.

### B. Infrared (IR) Range Sensors
- **Principle:** Triangulation using reflected IR light beam.
- A PSD (Position Sensitive Detector) measures the angle of reflected light → distance is inversely computed.
- **Pros:** Fast, low cost, compact.
- **Cons:** Affected by ambient light, color, and reflectivity of target.

### C. Laser Range Sensors (LiDAR / Time-of-Flight Laser)
- **Principle:** Time-of-Flight of laser pulse or phase-shift measurement.
- **Pros:** Very high accuracy (±1 mm), long range (up to 200 m), narrow beam.
- **Cons:** Expensive, eye-safety concerns (Class 1/2 lasers used).

### D. Capacitive Range Sensors
- **Principle:** Change in capacitance between sensor plates as object approaches (dielectric change).
- **Used for:** Detecting non-metallic objects, level sensing in tanks.

### E. Inductive Range Sensors
- **Principle:** Generation of eddy currents in metallic targets; loss in oscillator amplitude.
- **Used for:** Short-range (<30 mm) detection of metal objects only.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** In autonomous vehicles (e.g., mobile robots like KUKA's KMR), LiDAR provides 360° environmental mapping. In industrial pick-and-place, ultrasonic sensors provide low-cost obstacle detection on conveyor belts.

---

## 2.2 Sniff Sensors — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

A **sniff sensor (Electronic Nose)** is a multi-stage system:

1. **Sampling System** — Draws ambient air across the sensor array.
2. **Sensor Array** — Multiple gas sensors (SnO₂, ZnO, conducting polymers, MOS, electrochemical).
3. **Signal Conditioning** — Amplifies and digitizes the sensor responses.
4. **Pattern Recognition Engine** — PCA, LDA, or Neural Network to classify the gas.
5. **Output Decision** — "Identified: Methanol, Concentration: 350 ppm."

### Sensor Sub-Types:
- **MOS (Metal-Oxide Semiconductor):** e.g., MQ-3 (alcohol), MQ-7 (CO), TGS-2602 (VOCs).
- **Electrochemical:** Amperometric, measures current from redox reaction.
- **PID (Photoionization Detector):** Uses UV lamp to ionize VOCs.
- **IR Spectroscopy-based:** Non-dispersive infrared (NDIR) — used in CO₂ sniffers.

---

## 2.3 Robot End Effectors — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

The end effector is **interfaced to the robot wrist** via a **mechanical coupling plate** (ISO 9409 standard flange). The end effector has three functional requirements:

1. **Mechanical Coupling** — Rigid attachment to the wrist.
2. **Actuation Interface** — Power and signal lines (pneumatic, electrical, data).
3. **Task-Specific Tooling** — The actual gripping or working element.

### Design Considerations:
- **Payload:** Must match robot's rated payload.
- **Accuracy/Repeatability:** ±0.02 mm for precision assembly, ±1 mm for heavy picking.
- **Cycle Time:** <1 sec for high-speed pick-and-place.
- **Environment:** IP67 for dusty/wet, ATEX for explosive atmospheres.

---

## 2.4 Mechanical Grippers — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

The **gripping force** generated by a mechanical gripper must satisfy two engineering constraints:

1. **Sufficient Force to Prevent Slippage:** $$F_{grip} \geq \frac{m \cdot g}{\mu \cdot n}$$
   where `m` is object mass, `g` is gravity, `μ` is friction coefficient, `n` is number of contact points.
2. **Not Excessive to Avoid Deformation:** $$F_{grip} \leq F_{allowable}$$

### Gripper Design Process:
1. Identify the workpiece geometry, mass, and surface.
2. Calculate minimum gripping force.
3. Select the actuation system.
4. Design the finger geometry (length, shape, contact pad).
5. Add compliance (springs, compliance devices) to absorb positional errors.

---

## 2.5 Types of Gripper Mechanism — Detailed Analysis

### A. Pneumatic Grippers
- **Force source:** Compressed air acting on a piston.
- **Gripping Force:** $$F_{grip} = P \cdot A_{piston} \cdot \eta \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$
  where `P` is air pressure, `A` is piston area, `η` is mechanical efficiency, `α` is the wedge angle of the jaw linkage.
- **Use Case:** High-speed, clean environments, food/pharma industry.

### B. Hydraulic Grippers
- **Force source:** Hydraulic oil pressure (up to 210 bar).
- **Use Case:** Heavy payload (>20 kg), forging, casting, automotive.

### C. Servo-Electric (Motorized) Grippers
- **Force source:** DC servo/brushless motor + lead screw/belt.
- **Use Case:** Precise force control, variable grip width, cleanroom (no air exhaust).

### D. Magnetic Grippers
- **Electromagnetic Grippers:** Use a DC-energized coil; require continuous power.
- **Permanent Magnet Grippers:** Use a movable plunger to switch flux path on/off.
- **Use Case:** Ferromagnetic parts only — sheet metal, steel plates.

### E. Vacuum (Suction) Grippers
- **Principle:** Bernoulli's equation / Venturi effect / vacuum pump.
- **Lifting Force:** $$F_{lift} = \Delta P \cdot A_{cup}$$
  where `ΔP` is pressure differential (typically −0.6 to −0.9 bar), `A` is the effective cup area.
- **Use Case:** Smooth, non-porous flat surfaces — glass, cardboard, sheet metal, plastic.

### F. Adhesive Grippers
- **Inspired by:** Gecko lizard foot pads.
- **Mechanism:** Van der Waals / micro-suction via elastomer micro-pillars.
- **Use Case:** Space applications, clean glass handling.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Typical Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Ultrasonic distance from ToF | c = **343 m/s**, t in seconds, d in m |
| 2 | $F_{grip} \geq \dfrac{m \cdot g}{\mu \cdot n}$ | Minimum grip force (no slip) | F in N, m in kg, μ ≈ 0.1–0.6 |
| 3 | $F_{grip} = P \cdot A \cdot \eta \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ | Pneumatic gripper output force | P in Pa, A in m² |
| 4 | $F_{lift} = \Delta P \cdot A_{cup}$ | Vacuum cup lifting force | ΔP in Pa, A in m² |
| 5 | $V = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot s$ | Piston displacement (cylinder) | D = bore, s = stroke |
| 6 | $\tau_{T} = F \cdot r$ | Motor torque to actuate finger (F at radius r) | τ in Nm |
| 7 | $P_{grip} = \dfrac{F_{grip}}{A_{contact}}$ | Contact pressure on object | Pa |
| 8 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | Gripper mechanical efficiency | Dimensionless, 0.6–0.9 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** Memorize formulas 1, 2, 3, and 4 — these appear in **at least one numerical question** in every End Semester Examination under the OEMRT721 course.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application Domain | Sensor/Gripper Type | Example Use |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive Assembly | Pneumatic + Servo-Electric grippers | Door panel installation, engine block pick-up |
| Food Industry | Vacuum + Soft grippers | Cake, fruit, bread handling |
| Pharma/Cleanroom | Servo-Electric grippers | Vial handling, sterile pick-and-place |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ultrasonic Range Sensor Distance Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU OEMRT721 Module 2 examinations.

**Given:** An ultrasonic transmitter emits a pulse at time `t₁`. The pulse travels to an object at distance `d`, reflects, and is received at time `t₂`.

**Step 1 — Write the time of flight of the echo:**
$$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1$$

This is the **total round-trip time** taken by the acoustic pulse to travel from the sensor → object → back to the sensor.

**Step 2 — Apply the fundamental distance-time-velocity relationship:**

For a one-way trip, the distance `d` is related to speed `c` and time `t_one\_way` by:
$$d = c \cdot t_{one\_way}$$

**Step 3 — Express the one-way time in terms of the round-trip time:**

Since the pulse travels the same distance `d` twice (forward and back), the total distance is `2d`:
$$2d = c \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for `d`:**
$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

This is the **final ultrasonic distance equation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Substitution of c:** At standard atmospheric conditions (T = 20°C, sea level), the speed of sound in air is **c = 343 m/s**. For higher precision, use the temperature-corrected formula: `c = 331 + 0.6T` where T is in °C.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Ultrasonic Sensor)

> **Problem:** An ultrasonic range sensor on a mobile robot emits a pulse at `t = 0 ms`. The echo returns at `t = 12 ms`. If the speed of sound is **343 m/s**, calculate the distance to the obstacle.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**
$$\Delta t = 12 \text{ ms} = 12 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}, \quad c = 343 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the distance equation:**
$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**
$$d = \frac{343 \times 12 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**
$$343 \times 12 = 4116 \Rightarrow 4116 \times 10^{-3} = 4.116 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Divide by 2:**
$$d = \frac{4.116}{2} = 2.058 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** $d \approx \mathbf{2.06 \text{ m}}$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Gripping Force (No-Slip Condition)

**Given:** A mechanical gripper holds a workpiece of mass `m` against gravity using two fingers with coefficient of friction `μ` between finger pad and object.

**Step 1 — Force balance on the object (vertical equilibrium):**

The total upward friction force from both fingers must balance the weight:
$$2 \cdot F_{friction} = m \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Express friction force using Coulomb's friction law:**
$$F_{friction} = \mu \cdot F_{grip}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**
$$2 \cdot \mu \cdot F_{grip} = m \cdot g$$

**Step 4 — Solve for `F_grip`:**
$$F_{grip} = \frac{m \cdot g}{2\mu}$$

> **Generalized (for n fingers):**
$$\boxed{F_{grip} = \frac{m \cdot g}{n \cdot \mu}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Gripping Force)

> **Problem:** A 2-finger gripper must hold a 2 kg steel block. The coefficient of friction between rubber finger pads and steel is `μ = 0.4`. Calculate the minimum gripping force per finger. Use `g = 9.81 m/s²`.

**Step 1 — Variables:**
$$m = 2 \text{ kg}, \quad \mu = 0.4, \quad n = 2, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply formula:**
$$F_{grip} = \frac{m \cdot g}{n \cdot \mu}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**
$$F_{grip} = \frac{2 \times 9.81}{2 \times 0.4} = \frac{19.62}{0.8}$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**
$$F_{grip} = 24.525 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Apply safety factor (typical industry SF = 2 to 3):**
$$F_{design} = SF \times F_{grip} = 2 \times 24.525 = 49.05 \text{ N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The design grip force is **2× to 3× the minimum** to account for vibration, dynamic loading, and friction uncertainty. Students who forget the safety factor **lose 2 marks** in the board exam.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Pneumatic Gripper Output Force

**Step 1 — Pneumatic pressure `P` acts on piston area `A`:**
$$F_{piston} = P \cdot A$$

**Step 2 — Wedge/linkage mechanism converts linear piston force to tangential jaw force at angle `α`:**
$$F_{jaw} = \frac{F_{piston}}{\tan(\alpha)}$$

**Step 3 — Total gripping force (both jaws sum):**
$$F_{grip} = 2 \cdot F_{jaw} \cdot \eta$$

**Step 4 — Substituting:**
$$\boxed{F_{grip} = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot A \cdot \eta}{\tan(\alpha)}}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Sensor vs. Gripper Comparison

| Parameter | Range Sensor (Ultrasonic) | Range Sensor (LiDAR) | Sniff Sensor | Mechanical Gripper | Vacuum Gripper |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Acoustic ToF | Optical ToF / Phase-shift | Chemical adsorption | Mechanical jaw closure | Pressure differential |
| **Output Type** | Distance (m) | Point cloud | Gas ID + ppm | Force (N) + Position | Lifting Force (N) |
| **Range** | 0.05–10 m | 0.1–200 m | 1–1000 ppm | 0–500 mm stroke | 0.1–50 kg payload |
| **Accuracy** | ±10 mm | ±1 mm | ±5% of reading | ±0.05 mm | ±2 mm |
| **Cost (USD)** | $5–50 | $500–50,000 | $100–5,000 | $200–10,000 | $50–2,000 |
| **Target Restriction** | None | Reflectivity-dependent | Gas-specific | Geometric | Smooth, non-porous |
| **Cycle Time** | 50 ms | 100 ms | 1–10 s | 0.2–2 s | 0.5–3 s |
| **IP Rating Required** | IP65 | IP67 | IP54 | IP65 | IP65 |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High | Medium | Low | **Very High** | High |
