| **RS-232 Max Distance** | $\text{D}_{max} = 15 \, \text{m}$ | At 19.2 kbps (EIA-232) |
| **RS-485 Max Nodes** | $\text{N}_{max} = 32$ units/load | Excluding repeaters |
| **RS-485 Max Distance** | $\text{D}_{max} = 1200 \, \text{m}$ | At ≤ 100 kbps |
| **Baud Rate vs Distance** | $\text{Rate} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{Distance}}$ | Inverse relationship |
| **Cable Length from Baud** | $\text{L}_{max} \, (\text{m}) \approx \dfrac{10^8}{\text{Baud Rate} \times 10}$ | Empirical |
| **Voltage Threshold (RS-232)** | $\vert V \vert > 3 \, \text{V}$ | Defines logic level |
| **Parity Bit (Even)** | $P = \sum \text{data bits} \pmod{2} = 0$ | Error detection |
| **Data Frame Size** | $\text{F} = \text{Start} + \text{Data} + \text{Parity} + \text{Stop}$ | Bits |
| **CRC-16 (Modbus)** | $\text{Polynomial} = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^{2} + 1$ | Hex: 0xA001 |
| **Signal Propagation Time** | $t_p = \dfrac{L \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** PLC-to-Computer communication is the **backbone of Industry 4.0**. It enables SCADA dashboards, predictive maintenance via cloud uplinks, and the data acquisition necessary for digital twin simulations in smart factories.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Cable Length as a Function of Baud Rate

The empirical relationship between reliable cable length and baud rate for RS-485 follows the inverse proportionality rule. Starting from the standard 10 Mbps at 12 m, we can derive the maximum distance for any target baud rate.

**Given:**
- Reference pair: $R_0 = 10 \, \text{Mbps}$ at $L_0 = 12 \, \text{m}$.

**Step 1:** State the inverse proportionality relation.

$$L \cdot R = L_0 \cdot R_0 = k \, (\text{constant})$$

**Step 2:** Solve for maximum length at any baud rate.

$$L_{max} = \dfrac{L_0 \cdot R_0}{R}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute reference values.

$$L_{max} = \dfrac{12 \, \text{m} \times 10 \times 10^{6} \, \text{bps}}{R}$$

**Step 4:** Compute for a target baud rate of $R = 9600 \, \text{bps}$.

$$L_{max} = \dfrac{12 \times 10^{7}}{9600} = 12500 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 5:** Apply practical engineering limit. The RS-485 standard physically caps cable length at $1200 \, \text{m}$ due to signal attenuation and reflection, so:

$$L_{max} \, (\text{practical}) = \min \left( \dfrac{12 \times 10^7}{R}, \; 1200 \right) \, \text{m}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Frame Transmission Time per Modbus RTU Message

For a Modbus RTU Read Holding Registers request, the standard frame is 8 bytes: `[Addr][Func][StartHi][StartLo][QtyHi][QtyLo][CRCLo][CRCHi]`.

**Step 1:** Total bits in one frame.

$$N_{bits} = 8 \, \text{bytes} \times 11 \, \text{bits/byte}$$

The "11 bits" comes from 1 start + 8 data + 1 parity (even) + 1 stop = **11 bits per byte**.

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$N_{bits} = 8 \times 11 = 88 \, \text{bits}$$

**Step 3:** Compute transmission time at $B = 19200 \, \text{bps}$.

$$t_{tx} = \dfrac{N_{bits}}{B} = \dfrac{88}{19200}$$

$$t_{tx} = 4.583 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s} = 4.583 \, \text{ms}$$

**Step 4:** Include the 3.5 character silence gap (Modbus RTU inter-frame delimiter).

$$t_{gap} = \dfrac{3.5 \times 11}{19200} = 2.005 \, \text{ms}$$

**Step 5:** Total round-trip time (ignoring propagation).

$$t_{total} = 2 \times (t_{tx} + t_{gap}) = 2 \times (4.583 + 2.005) = 13.18 \, \text{ms}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Throughput Calculation for a Multi-Drop Network

Consider **8 PLCs** polled cyclically by a master computer over RS-485 at **9600 bps**, each PLC requiring **1 Modbus RTU read** of 8 bytes per scan.

**Step 1:** Bits per request per PLC (from Section 3.2).

$$N = 88 \, \text{bits/PLC}$$

**Step 2:** Bits scanned per cycle for all PLCs.

$$N_{cycle} = 8 \times 88 = 704 \, \text{bits}$$

**Step 3:** Add inter-frame gaps per request.

$$t_{gap} = \dfrac{11 \times 3.5}{9600} = 4.01 \, \text{ms/request}$$

**Step 4:** Time per PLC poll.

$$t_{PLC} = \dfrac{88}{9600} + 4.01 \times 10^{-3} = 9.17 + 4.01 = 13.18 \, \text{ms}$$

**Step 5:** Total cycle time.

$$T_{cycle} = 8 \times 13.18 = 105.44 \, \text{ms} \approx 0.105 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 6:** System refresh rate.

$$f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cycle}} = \dfrac{1}{0.105} \approx 9.5 \, \text{Hz}$$

This means the SCADA dashboard updates every ~**105 ms**, suitable for most supervisory monitoring applications.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Interface | Max Distance | Max Devices | Typical Baud | Noise Immunity | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RS-232 | 15 m | 1 (point-to-point) | 115.2 kbps | **Low** | Low |
| RS-485 | 1200 m | 32 (multi-drop) | 10 Mbps | **High** (differential) | Medium |
| USB 2.0 | 5 m | 1 (point-to-point) | 480 Mbps | Medium | Low |
| Ethernet (TCP/IP) | 100 m/segment | Unlimited (via switches) | 1 Gbps | **High** | Medium-High |
| Fiber Optic | 2–40 km | Many | 1–10 Gbps | **Very High** | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Communication Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — PLC ↔ Computer Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["FIELD SENSORS AND ACTUATORS"] --> nodeB["PLC CPU WITH LADDER LOGIC"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["PLC COMMUNICATION PORT RJ45 OR DB9"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["PHYSICAL CABLE ETHERNET OR RS485"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["PROTOCOL CONVERTER OR DIRECT DRIVER"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["COMPUTER NETWORK INTERFACE CARD"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["PROGRAMMING SOFTWARE TIA PORTAL"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["APPLICATION LAYER PROGRAM UPLOAD OR MONITOR"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["SCADA OR HMI DASHBOARD"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["DATABASE AND DATA LOGGING"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["OPERATOR DECISION"]
    nodeK --> nodeG
```

### 4.2 Communication Flow Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Operator Initiates Connect Command"] --> stepB["PC Sends ENQ Enquiry Frame"]
    stepB --> stepC{"PLC Receives Valid Frame"}
    stepC -->|"Yes"| stepD["PLC Sends ACK Acknowledgement"]
    stepC -->|"No"| stepE["PLC Discards Frame Timeout"]
    stepD --> stepF["PC Sends Read or Write Request"]
    stepF --> stepG["PLC Processes Request"]
    stepG --> stepH["PLC Sends Response Data and CRC"]
    stepH --> stepI["PC Validates CRC and Parses Data"]
    stepI -->|"CRC Valid"| stepJ["Data Displayed in Software"]
    stepI -->|"CRC Error"| stepK["Request Retransmission"]
```

### 4.3 Multi-Drop Network Topology (RS-485 / Modbus)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    masterNode["MASTER PC SCADA WITH RS485 ADAPTER"] --> busLine["RS485 TWISTED PAIR BUS"]
    busLine --> slave1["PLC NODE 1 ADDRESS 01"]
    busLine --> slave2["PLC NODE 2 ADDRESS 02"]
    busLine --> slave3["PLC NODE 3 ADDRESS 03"]
    busLine --> slaveN["PLC NODE N ADDRESS N MAX 32"]
    slave1 -.-> terminationR["TERMINATION RESISTOR 120 OHM AT BOTH ENDS"]
    slaveN -.-> terminationR
```

### 4.4 Layered Protocol Stack Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer7["LAYER 7 APPLICATION - LADDER LOGIC UPLOAD READ WRITE COIL"]
    layer6["LAYER 6 PRESENTATION - DATA ENCODING ASCII OR BINARY"]
    layer5["LAYER 5 SESSION - CONNECTION ESTABLISHMENT AND TIMEOUT"]
    layer4["LAYER 4 TRANSPORT - TCP FOR ETHERNET MODBUS"]
    layer3["LAYER 3 NETWORK - IP ADDRESSING AND ROUTING"]
    layer2["LAYER 2 DATA LINK - MODBUS RTU FRAMING WITH CRC16"]
    layer1["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL - RS485 OR ETHERNET RJ45"]
    layer7 --> layer6
    layer6 --> layer5
    layer5 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer1
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEMRT721]**
*List any three physical interfaces used to connect a PLC to a computer. Mention one limitation of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **RS-232:** Supports only point-to-point connection with a maximum cable length of **15 m**. (1 mark)
2. **RS-485:** Supports up to 32 devices but requires termination resistors at both ends to prevent signal reflection. (1 mark)
3. **USB:** Limited to ~5 m and point-to-point; not suitable for industrial long-distance multi-drop networks. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEMRT721]**
*What is the role of the PPI (Point-to-Point Interface) protocol in Siemens PLCs?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- PPI is a Siemens proprietary **master-slave protocol** used primarily for communication with the **S7-200 series** PLC. (1 mark)
- It operates on **RS-485 physical layer** and allows one master (PC) to communicate with multiple slave PLCs up to **32 nodes**. (1 mark)
- The default baud rate is **9600 bps**, and it uses a token-passing mechanism for deterministic access. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam Pattern]*

**Q. (a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for connecting a Siemens S7-200 PLC to a personal computer using a PC/PPI cable. Include the required software configuration parameters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Hardware Connection:** The PC/PPI cable converts the **USB port of the PC** to the **RS-485 port of the S7-200 PLC**. The cable contains an onboard electronic converter and is powered by the USB port. (1 mark)

2. **Software Installation:** Install **STEP 7 Micro/WIN** programming software on the PC. (1 mark)

3. **Set PC/PPI Cable DIP Switches:** Configure the cable to operate at the correct baud rate (e.g., 9600 bps) and select the "PPI" communication mode. (1 mark)

4. **Communication Setup in Software:** Open STEP 7 Micro/WIN → Go to "Communications" menu → Set "PC/PPI cable (PPI)" as the interface. Set the baud rate to **9600 bps** and the highest station address to **31**. (1 mark)

5. **Double-Click "Double-Click to Refresh":** The software will scan the PPI network and detect the connected PLC with its address. (1 mark)

6. **Establish Online Link:** Click "OK" to connect. Once online, the user can **upload, download, and monitor** the ladder logic. (1 mark)

7. **Verify Communication:** Test by toggling a known output bit in monitor mode and observing the LED on the PLC. (1 mark)

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the OSI-based communication model between a PLC and a SCADA computer over Modbus TCP/IP. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    scadaA["SCADA PC APPLICATION LAYER"] --> tcpA["TRANSPORT TCP LAYER"]
    tcpA --> ipA["NETWORK IP LAYER"]
    ipA --> ethA["DATA LINK ETHERNET MAC LAYER"]
    ethA --> phyA["PHYSICAL RJ45 TWISTED PAIR"]
    phyA -.-> switchNode["INDUSTRIAL ETHERNET SWITCH"]
    switchNode -.-> phyB["PHYSICAL RJ45 TWISTED PAIR"]
    phyB --> ethB["DATA LINK ETHERNET MAC LAYER"]
    ethB --> ipB["NETWORK IP LAYER"]
    ipB --> tcpB["TRANSPORT TCP LAYER"]
    tcpB --> modbusB["MODBUS APPLICATION LAYER"]
    modbusB --> plcB["PLC CPU WITH PROCESS DATA"]
```

**2. Layer-wise Explanation (4 marks):**

- **Application Layer (Modbus):** Defines the function codes — FC03 (Read Holding Registers), FC06 (Write Single Register), FC16 (Write Multiple Registers). (1 mark)
- **Transport Layer (TCP):** Modbus TCP uses port **502**. TCP ensures reliable, ordered, error-checked delivery. (1 mark)
- **Network Layer (IP):** Each device is assigned a unique IP address (e.g., PLC: 192.168.0.10, SCADA: 192.168.0.20). Routing and packet fragmentation are handled here. (1 mark)
- **Data Link + Physical Layer (Ethernet):** MAC addressing, CSMA/CD access, RJ45 connectors, Cat 5e/Cat 6 cables, max 100 m per segment. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice]*

**Q. (a)** Compare RS-232 and RS-485 communication standards in terms of voltage levels, topology, maximum distance, and noise immunity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | RS-232 | RS-485 |
|---|---|---|
| **Voltage Level** | $\pm 12 \, \text{V}$ (single-ended) | Differential $\pm 1.5$ to $\pm 5 \, \text{V}$ |
| **Topology** | Point-to-point (1-to-1) | Multi-drop (up to 32 nodes) |
| **Max Distance** | 15 m | 1200 m |
| **Max Speed** | 115.2 kbps | 10 Mbps |
| **Noise Immunity** | Low (single-ended) | High (differential) |
| **Connectors** | DB9 / DB25 | Screw terminals / RJ45 |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium |

(Each correct comparison point = 1 mark; 7 marks for full table)

---

**Q. (b)** A factory has 10 PLCs connected via RS-485 Modbus RTU at 19200 bps. Each PLC requires one read holding register request (8 bytes). Calculate the total cycle time and the system refresh rate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Bits per byte (8-N-1 format with even parity).
$$\text{Bits/byte} = 1 (\text{start}) + 8 (\text{data}) + 1 (\text{parity}) + 1 (\text{stop}) = 11$$

**Step 2:** Bits per request.
$$N = 8 \times 11 = 88 \, \text{bits}$$

**Step 3:** Time per request at 19200 bps.
$$t_{req} = \dfrac{88}{19200} = 4.583 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s} = 4.583 \, \text{ms}$$

**Step 4:** Inter-frame silent gap (3.5 character times).
$$t_{gap} = \dfrac{3.5 \times 11}{19200} = 2.005 \, \text{ms}$$

**Step 5:** Time per PLC poll.
$$t_{PLC} = 4.583 + 2.005 = 6.588 \, \text{ms}$$

**Step 6:** Total cycle time for 10 PLCs.
$$T_{cycle} = 10 \times 6.588 = 65.88 \, \text{ms}$$

**Step 7:** System refresh rate.
$$f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cycle}} = \dfrac{1}{65.88 \times 10^{-3}} = 15.18 \, \text{Hz}$$

**Final Answer:** Total cycle time = **65.88 ms**; System refresh rate ≈ **15.18 Hz**. (Final answer 1 mark, all steps 6 marks)

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall 1:** Students often confuse the *baud rate* with the *bit rate*. For Modbus RTU, **1 character = 11 bits**, so the effective data throughput is much lower than the baud rate number. Expecting "19200 bps = 19200 data bits/s" loses **2 marks**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall 2:** When asked about "PLC communication," students write only about the cable and forget the **software configuration** step (driver, address, baud rate matching). Examiners explicitly mark the *software side* — losing 2–3 marks if omitted.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall 3:** Students frequently write "RS-485 is faster than Ethernet" — this is factually wrong. RS-485's 10 Mbps is its *physical* limit; Ethernet commonly runs at 1 Gbps (100× faster). A 14-mark question on protocols is **automatic 4-mark loss** for such an error.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall 4:** Skipping the **CRC error-checking** step in the description of Modbus RTU results in losing 1 mark in part (a) of 7-mark questions. Always mention "**16-bit Cyclic Redundancy Check**" explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PLC-to-Computer communication** requires **three coordinated layers**: physical (cable), protocol (language), and software (translator).
- **RS-232** = short-range, point-to-point, $\pm 12 \, \text{V}$, max **15 m**, single-ended (poor noise immunity).
- **RS-485** = long-range, multi-drop (32 nodes), max **1200 m**, differential signaling (high noise immunity).
- **USB** is for direct, short-range programming of modern compact PLCs.
- **Industrial Ethernet** (Modbus TCP, EtherNet/IP, Profinet) is the modern Industry 4.0 standard — uses RJ45, port **502** for Modbus.
- **PPI** = Siemens S7-200 proprietary protocol over RS-485 at default **9600 bps**.
- **Modbus RTU** frame = 1 byte address + 1 byte function + N data + 2 bytes CRC16. Silent gap = **3.5 character times**.
- **Baud Rate vs Distance:** Inversely proportional — doubling distance halves the maximum safe baud rate.
- **CRC-16** polynomial for Modbus = $x^{16} + x^{15} + x^2 + 1$ (hex **0xA001**).
- **Termination resistors (120 Ω)** must be installed at both physical ends of an RS-485 bus to suppress signal reflection.
- **Cycle Time Calculation:** $T_{cycle} = N \times \left( \dfrac{11 \times L_{msg}}{B} + \dfrac{3.5 \times 11}{B} \right)$ where $L_{msg}$ = message bytes, $B$ = baud rate, $N$ = number of polled nodes.
- **Refresh Rate:** $f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cycle}}$.
- **Always configure** baud rate, parity, data bits, and station address identically on **both** PC software and PLC firmware.
- **KTU Favorite Question Pattern:** "Explain the procedure to connect a PLC to a PC" — emphasize hardware **AND** software steps, then list communication parameters explicitly.
